column64: I am from Chile and moved to the US in 2015 when I was 35.
column66: I was born and raised in Santiago, Chile, in a family of teachers: my parents and grandparents were school teachers. I grew up in a house full of books, thus full of stories. My father and my grandmother liked to sing and laugh. My mother kept us (my sisters and me) focused on our education, and she was a model of what a working, independent woman can achieve. I graduated as a Social Anthropologist and started working at the Ministry of Women in different roles and programs dedicated to supporting women's economic and personal independence and growth. I went back to academia to pursue a Master's degree in Sociology. In 2014, I fell in love with a charming American man, moved to the US, and started again. When you move to another country, you are nobody, and you need to prove to yourself and others the value in yourself - because your experience does not get lost when crossing the border. I started volunteering in different organizations: I worked with refugees from the Middle East, Africa, and Latin America, and I could apply my abilities to connect two different cultures and make that process less rough for the newcomers. Nine years later, I have rebuilt myself by creating spaces to continue working on achieving equity and social justice for women and their communities. Currently, I am a Project Manager for the Uplift Central Coast Coalition, which aims to improve access to quality jobs based on equity and sustainability efforts for the California Central Coast residents.
column67: I was a guest speaker for one of the groups, and I enjoyed the discussion and the participants' questions. I believe sharing my experience as an immigrant professional woman in the US can be a positive.
column68: How to build a career following your heart. I am a Social Anthropologist, so I base my experience on learning about the society I live in and its diversity.
column69: Connection to new generations and understanding what they think and believe. I also want to add a new personal experience and development.
column70: Project management involves working with people from diverse backgrounds, using workforce development tools, public speaking, and critical thinking.
column71: When I finished college, I was not sure about where I could work. I did not have a network to start volunteering or as an intern.

column66: I'm Chris Herrera, originally from Allentown, PA. I honed my design skills studying Graphic Design at Lehigh University, where I now proudly work for the College of Arts and Sciences. column67: I would like to become a SPEAKHIRE Champion because I resonate with the core mission of the organization. As a child of immigrants myself and first-generation college student, I have a lived experience of trying to pursue a degree and career with little resources and knowledge. However, through hard work and the help of many people along the way I have managed to build my own path and career doing what I love. I also obtained a master's degree in Educational Leadership and while in that program I actually learned about cultural and social capital, writing several papers on the topic. This knowledge has impacted my life and guides my mindset on how I interact with people and even how I parent my own children.
column68: In my career as a designer in higher education I have learned a great deal about academia and the different routes available to students. I would love to share my own experience and the path I took to help give students insight into what is possible. I also am very passionate about my career as a designer. I can share what skills I use on a daily basis, how I problem solve, how I work within a team, and how I solve problems for stakeholders. I would love to show students examples of how rewarding my career in design is.
column69: I am a believer in paying things forward. As a first-generation student, there were so many things about academia and higher-education that were unknown to me. However, I had great people and mentors along the way that offered their help to me in the form of advice, encouragement, and imparting of skills. I had professors in community college who saw I just needed a gentle push, and words of validation. It was the missing ingredient I needed to believe in myself and the talent I had within. I am grateful these people took the time and cared enough to help me. So, at this point in my career I would love to do the same for young students who look like me and come from a similar background.
column70: Some examples that come to mind that I can speak to as a designer is the ability to listen, collaborate, and creative problem solve. I feel that these are skills I use on a regular basis. For instance, when designing websites for the College I need to gather and listen to stakeholder feedback. This feedback is valuable data that can help inform decision making. I'll collaborate with my colleagues and see how my design decisions may impact the engineering and web development side of things. Ultimately, any web design that I create uses problem solving skills as I need to determine if it meets the needs of the end user, the needs of the stakeholders, and meets engineering standards.
column71: As a first-generation college student I wasn't aware of the different types of careers available to me. I did what I thought my parents wanted of me. However, that left me feeling empty and led me to the wrong path early in my academic career. It took me two years to realize I needed to switch to design. Ultimately, it took me transferring to community college to realize I belonged in design. I obtained my associates degree and transferred again to complete my bachelor's degree. I also worked while pursing my degree so that also presented different challenges in terms of needing to manage my time wisely. Ultimately, it took me 8 years to obtain my bachelor's degree. It took longer than I would have liked but I navigated it the best way I knew how at the time with the information and resources I had available.

column66: I?m a Biologist and conservation professional, and I currently live in Veracruz, Mexico. I have a Masters in Environmental Science, Policy and Management from the University of Manchester and Central European University. Since my undergrad I focused mostly on environmental education although I didn?t know what an important role it would play in my professional future. During grad school I spent two years living and studying in Hungary, Greece, UK, and South Africa. I conducted my MSc dissertation research in rural communities neighboring Kruger National Park, studying trade of firewood amongst communities. Later I spent a year working at the Ramsar convention secretariat in Switzerland, working on conservation of wetlands all across the Americas. For the past 6 years I have worked in sustainable tourism, conservation and experiential education, working with rural and indigenous communities, NGOs, government representatives, and students. Even though I was a middle school teacher for a year and thought it wasn?t a good fit, I recently discovered my passion for education, realizing how important it is that young people get a comprehensive picture of different realities, their challenges, and what a huge role they can play in generating solutions.

column66: My name is Patrick Burris, and I am a Learning and Development professional from Columbus, Ohio. I graduated with a Bachelor's in Human Resources Management from Franklin University, and I currently work as a Learning and Development Generalist in a consulting role. I believe that the best way to grow is to be a sponge; learn as much as you can, have as many experiences as you can, and don't be afraid to change directions if your career takes you that way! I'm excited to meet each and every one of you!
column67: Overall, I would like to become a SPEAKHIRE Champion because I would like to make a difference. During my development as a professional, I was helped by many amazing people along the way who gave me a variety of experiences and kind advice that shaped my career and who I am today. I believe that as a SPEAKHIRE Champion, I would have the opportunity to \"pay it forward\" and help be someone who can provide help and advice to those who need it.
column68: As a Champion, I hope to impart the knowledge that your career is a journey; some people know exactly what they want to do right out of the gate. They graduate high school and go on to the endeavors they wanted to. But for many of us, it doesn't work that way - as you go through life you have experiences that build on each other and continue to help you grow. I think that as a Champion, I would focus on the idea that what is most important is a growth mindset; you should continue to learn and grow and these things will define your career. Get involved in a new project at your job, help someone else out that has a different role, or ask your HR manager if you can learn a bit about what they do.
column69: I want to gain a few things from this experience. First and foremost, I genuinely want to have an opportunity to give back to the community, which would give me a sense of fulfillment. I would also like to professionally network and meet new people. I would have the opportunity meet students, professionals, and other Champions, and have the opportunity to learn from them.
column70: My interns could learn skills in professional development and networking from me. I was someone who graduated from high school and was unsure of where my career path would take me, but I kept an open mind and gathered as many experiences and met as many people as I could, which shaped my career. As a L&D professional, I could also teach skills in facilitation, content writing and development, communication, training, and leadership.
column71: As I have mentioned in other responses, when I was initially starting out I didn't know where my career would lead me. Originally, I studied to become a teacher, but while I was happy with idea of teaching, the profession wasn't a good fit for me. I always knew that HR was often responsible for training, but I didn't really realize there was a whole career path in Learning and Development. So, I studied Human Resources and planned for a career there, while I returned to school working full-time. Along the way, I had several experiences in HR-related jobs that pointed me in the direction of being in Learning and Development, and I ultimately ended up in this career field, loved it, and haven't looked back.

Cultural competency: With my extensive travel experience and global connections, I can help interns develop cultural awareness and sensitivity, which is essential in today's diverse workplace. Leadership: As a project manager and mentor, I can teach interns how to inspire and motivate others, delegate tasks, and take responsibility for their actions. Adaptability: Traveling the world has taught me the importance of being flexible and adaptable, skills that are critical in any career. Hard skills: Teaching: As an English Language Teacher, I can teach interns effective techniques for lesson planning, classroom management, and assessment. Project management: With my experience as a project manager, I can teach interns how to plan and execute projects effectively, manage timelines and budgets, and work collaboratively with team members. Music production: As a Grammy-nominated musician, I can teach interns the technical skills involved in music production, such as recording, mixing, and mastering. Networking: As a global connector, I can teach interns how to build relationships with people from different backgrounds and cultures, and how to leverage those connections for professional opportunities. Overall, interns can learn a wide range of soft and hard skills from me, from effective communication and cultural competency to project management and music production. These skills are valuable in a variety of fields and can help interns achieve success in their future careers.
column71: As an English Language Teacher, I faced challenges such as language barriers, cultural differences, and adapting to different teaching environments. As a project manager, I faced obstacles such as budget constraints, time management, and coordinating team members. As a grammy nominated musician, I faced challenges such as competition in the industry, creative blocks, and balancing artistic vision with commercial viability. And as a global connector, I faced obstacles such as building and maintaining relationships across different time zones and cultures, and navigating cultural nuances and communication styles. Another obstacle I faced throughout my career journey was self-doubt and uncertainty about which career path to pursue. It took time and effort to develop confidence in my abilities and find my true passion. This is a common challenge that many young people face, and I can offer guidance and support to help them navigate through these uncertainties and discover their own strengths and interests.

column66: Saima Malik is the Vice President of Human Resources and Recruiting at Avantus Federal. She brings 16 years of human resources experience within the private sector. She also leads the efforts around attracting and retaining the firm?s innovative workforce. In addition, Saima serves at the Ethics and Compliance Officer for Avantus? Ethics and Compliance program. Throughout her career, Saima has had proven success in leading human resources capabilities to include benefits, employee relations, performance management, compliance, and recruiting. Prior to joining Avantus, one of her primary responsibilities was to assist small government contractors in effectively building HR strategies, and in some cases, building entire HR departments from the ground up. Over the last several years, Saima has strived to cultivate a philosophy in which HR is no longer simply a process or reactive solution to the company?s talent, but is instead a proactive strategic partner that assists the organization in achieving its goals. In doing so, a primary focus of her career has become the ability to provide organizations with business strategies that are both efficient and effective. Saima holds a Bachelor of Science in Global Business Management and is a member of the Society for Human Resource Management (SHRM). She is also a recipient of the HR Impact Award from Washington Business Journal ? Best Places to Work.

column66: I?m a Biologist and conservation professional, and I currently live in Veracruz, Mexico. I have a Masters in Environmental Science, Policy and Management from the University of Manchester and Central European University. Since my undergrad I focused mostly on environmental education although I didn?t know what an important role it would play in my professional future. During grad school I spent two years living and studying in Hungary, Greece, UK, and South Africa. I conducted my MSc dissertation research in rural communities neighboring Kruger National Park, studying trade of firewood amongst communities. Later I spent a year working at the Ramsar convention secretariat in Switzerland, working on conservation of wetlands all across the Americas. For the past 6 years I have worked in sustainable tourism, conservation and experiential education, working with rural and indigenous communities, NGOs, government representatives, and students. Even though I was a middle school teacher for a year and thought it wasn?t a good fit, I recently discovered my passion for education, realizing how important it is that young people get a comprehensive picture of different realities, their challenges, and what a huge role they can play in generating solutions.

column67: I think it would be an amazing experience to inspire and excite others that anything is possible when you set your mind to it. I have challenged myself throughout my life to be better and do better. I have accomplished many incredible feats that I still have to pinch myself to check that this is now my reality! I have my doctorate in a profession, Nursing, that I truly love. I am lucky enough to be involved in Women's Services and I started my own consulting company, building my intellectual property. There are so many opportunities that I have been provided and although it took me a minute to get myself headed in the right direction, I made it! #orgullosadeserlatina #befearless #nursesrock
column68: The knowledge that if you set your mind to it, you can accomplish many things, even starting your own business and expanding your net worth.
column69: I want the opportunity to give back ~ my mentor and coach is the most amazing human I know and I want to pay it forward.
column70: Nursing is hard and as we have learned over the past three years, it is a profession that has lost its vision. However, there are so many things that one can do as a nurse, especially advocate for each other. There are many different areas of nursing pathways that one can take and that is an advantage of nurses that this profession has. Communication and advocacy of patients directly influences nursing and has allowed me to speak up in a manner I never thought I could before, not just for myself or my patients, but also for the nursing profession. Nurses are influencers both in the boardroom and in government.
column71: I was bullied and I was teased because I had dark skin and I spoke with an accent. My parents were first generation from Puerto Rico, we spoke Spanish at home, and we were raised differently from those who we lived by. I witnessed how my mom and dad were treated depending on where they were. It was very difficult and it made me want to ensure that I or my family members would not be treated any differently.

column66:
new bio: Irina Zilbergleyt is a Director, Investor Solutions of the Asset Management business of ORIX Corporation USA, responsible for product development, client coverage, capital raising and the expansion of the firm?s strategic partnerships to address the HNWI and RIA channels. She joined the firm in 2021 and is based in New York City. 

 

Ms. Zilbergleyt has over 15 years of experience in education, private equity and investment banking in US and Europe. Preceding her current role, Irina was a Director at IE University where she was responsible for design, implementation, and delivery of career-related curriculum for MBA, Masters and Undergraduate students.  Prior to IE, Ms. Zilbergleyt was Vice President at Ares Management where she evaluated, structured, and executed investments in real estate, private equity and fixed income asset classes. Prior to Ares, she was a Corporate Finance Analyst at Merrill Lynch where she participated in the execution of mergers and acquisitions as well as in transactions in the debt and equity capital markets.

 

Ms. Zilbergleyt holds a BA in Finance (summa cum laude) from the University of Illinois at Urbana Champaign.

column63:
Screening Notes by HJ 1/24/23: Was an educator so understands how to stay nimble for the instructional guide. Can be matched on STEM but also can be matched on understanding what an education career pathway could look like. Struggled to become financially stable, though, so has some negativity towards the field. Contributing to SPEAKHIRE because he wants to continue to give back. Wants to develop a diverse talent pipeline and mentioned a preference for girls, which alerted me a bit ? also asked how he can keep in touch with students after, what?s recommended, and I did say we suggest LinkedIn. Nothing alarming given his other responses and that he was a teacher for numerous years. Checked his online presence and things do checkout. Just mark for observation during sessions and check-in with students to see how things are going.

Spanish is limited and Korean even more so ? someone who speaks Spanish/Korean and a high level of English would benefit from matching with him as a non-language support Champion.
column66: Mikey Bedney was born and raised in Portland Oregon where he currently resides. He began his professional career by teaching math at an all girls private school in downtown Portland. During those 8 years his focus shifted toward tech as he built a computer science program that involved: designing new courses, coordinating women-in-tech panels, running week long tech events, and founding the school?s first tech club. The goal was to educate students on the benefits of careers in tech. As teaching demands led to an imbalanced life for himself, Mikey took a chance and enrolled in a 4 month evening bootcamp where he learned the basics of coding. It took roughly a year to find his first placement in tech (2016) and has been working as a software tester for several years. The opportunities in tech are many yet there are still too many misconceptions that turn people away before they truly understand what such a career offers. Forever an educator at heart, Mikey has continued to mentor all ages/backgrounds and always looks for ways to contribute his time, energy, and skills to diversify the tech workforce.
column67: Mentorship is at the core of who I am. Nothing can give more satisfaction than helping someone else succeed. In my high school teaching days I made it known that my passion for teaching (now in the form of mentoring) is what gave me fulfillment. I worked in an all-girls school and spearheaded an initiative of building tech/computer science offerings to young women and want to help diversify the workforce as the types of jobs are fantastic. So much is unknown about the field for high school students. I want to bridge that gap. STEM fields (specifically tech) has loads of opportunity, exciting challenges, and can actually provide a sustainable lifestyle.
column68: I want to paint a realistic picture of what tech is like and how to pursue a career in the field. If a high school student can truly understand the opportunities, benefits, etc of the field and THEN chooses not to follow the path---that would still be considered a win. There is joy to be had in this field and I want to share that with others.
column69: The satisfaction that can only come from helping another person succeed. I want to build long term relationships with youth who may enter the field and reach out throughout their lives when I can be of service to them.
column70: How to communicate with others, how to keep focused on life balance while pursuing any career opportunities, and how to connect with others in a meaningful way for those who want to some day mentor/lead others. I'd also hope to point them to things they can do now to continue to see if they are interested in going into the field of tech.
column71: Mainly financial---I had a degree (masters) in teaching which had me living paycheck to paycheck. I had to borrow money to go to a code school which has since be. The other was time. I had to juggle 3 jobs (1 was full time teaching) while going through the code school.

Screening Notes: I think Leigh Ann will be a great Champion. She has a lot of personal and professional experience. She grew up in a poor family and had to learn early on to work hard to achieve something in life. She grew up in a community with white people only and felt like she doesn?t really belong there. She wanted to get to know different cultures. She didn?t have a mentor in high school because she was only an average student with B?s and only A students with potential scholarships would get a mentor. She wished she had a mentor back then. She was told that her destiny would be working in a factory, but she did not want that and moved away. She got her degree, moved away to get more degrees, and started working. She moved around more often with her ex-husband who was in the military and recently moved back to El Paso, TX with her teenage son. She feels like she can bond with high school kids because of her profession as a teacher and her son. She already has some great ideas for the sessions wanting to make those sessions meaningful and interesting for the interns. CT 11/30/2022

column66: Dr. Wilson is currently Associate Professor of History & Communications at University of Massachusetts Global. She earned her Master's Degree in Journalism and Mass Communications at Kansas State University and her Ph.D. in History at the University of Memphis. Her research interests focus on US History, Latin American History, immigration, education, media law, and the history of communications. For 25 years, she has worked with people at every stage of their lifelong learning journeys, having taught English as a Second Language (ESL) to both children and adults, and taught GED training for ESL students and remedial and basic education for adults seeking to enter higher education classes. She has taught community college and university classes in History, Communications, Liberal Studies, English, and Humanities to adults since 1998.
column67: I love to work with people who are interested in finding ways to improve their lives. I have been a higher education professional for 25 years and one of the most rewarding aspects of teaching is working with students individually, learning what I can do to help them on their journeys. Although I am not an immigrant --- I'm as white as they get; my family came to the US in the 1700s --- I live on the US/Mexico border and taught English as a Second Language for years. I am also a specialist in immigration history, so I am very familiar with the historical plight of immigrants who have sought to enter the US. Now, as my city (El Paso, Texas), continues to seek ways to alleviate the suffering of those Venezuelan migrants who come here seeking asylum, becoming active with SpeakHire is one small way that I hope I can encourage others to dream big and never stop working for a better life.
column68: I want to share the knowledge that success is possible. Although I am a university professor and not an immigrant, according to US Census records, none of my grandparents got to the 7th grade in school. My maternal grandmother was barely literate. I grew up in the Deep South where education, self-improvement, and dreams of success were scorned. I was able to escape this mentality and give myself and my sons better lives. I want to encourage others and particularly immigrants, letting them know that there are people in the US who do want to see them succeed and want to help them on their journeys.
column69: I want to gain the satisfaction of getting to know more people who want to improve their lives. These immigrants come to the US seeking to do better and escape a dire situation. I want to support them and encourage them to work towards brighter futures.
column70: I work hard to be empathetic, particularly towards women with young children, teenagers/young adults who are survivors of abuse. As an immigration historian, I can use my background to help immigrants see that their situation is not hopeless. There have been others before them who have had difficult journeys to the US, but been successful. Also, for those who wish to improve their English skills, pass the TOEFL, work towards a GED, or other such formal credentials, I have the background to help them prepare for such rigorous tests.
column71: First, I was told when I was a teenager that I would never be anything. Although I was a B student, in high school, my guidance counselor told me that if I worked really hard, maybe I could get my cosmetology license and learn how to do hair. (Not that there's anything wrong with that at all. It's certainly a noble, honest profession.) Next, upon completion of my bachelor's degree, when I told my family that I wanted to go for a master's degree, they said that I could only do that as long as my then-husband approved. Then when I began the Ph.D. in History, I was told multiple times that this was a man's profession, I was too stupid to get into the Ph.D. program, I should not even have a high school diploma much less a Ph.D. (ie, "who let you in here?"), that I "slept my way" to good grades --- the list is endless and extremely painful, more than I care to revisit.

column66: Tina Loarte-Rodriguez, MSN, RN, CIC, CPPS, CPHRM is the Vice President of Nursing at Wheeler Health, a Federally Qualified Health Center with five integrated primary care locations in central Connecticut. Her priority is supporting the team in providing high quality, safe, patient centered care. She oversees the nursing department, medical assistant practice, and infection control at the Health and Wellness Centers, school and congregate care homes.    Her professional experience includes teaching middle school math for four years and over sixteen years in health care in perioperative and primary care nursing, performance improvement, infection control, risk management, and patient safety. A graduate from Cornell University, UCONN, and Quinnipiac University, she is certified in infection prevention and control, patient safety, and healthcare risk management.    She is an eternal learner with a passion for health equity and high reliability. Tina is a Hartford Business Journal Healthcare Hero Award winner and led the Wheeler nursing team to win the Ct Nursing Association Excellence in Team Award, both in 2021. An active member of the National Association of Hispanic Nurses- Hartford Chapter and the Ct Nursing Association General Assembly Committee, Nomination Committee and DEI Committee. Tina is committed to giving back and advocacy. She loves dancing, eating, reading, and traveling. Originally born and raised in the Bronx, NY, she now resides in CT with her husband, three children, her father, and two rescue dogs.

column67: I think it would be an amazing experience to inspire and excite others that anything is possible when you set your mind to it. I have challenged myself throughout my life to be better and do better. I have accomplished many incredible feats that I still have to pinch myself to check that this is now my reality! I have my doctorate in a profession, Nursing, that I truly love. I am lucky enough to be involved in Women's Services and I started my own consulting company, building my intellectual property. There are so many opportunities that I have been provided and although it took me a minute to get myself headed in the right direction, I made it! #orgullosadeserlatina #befearless #nursesrock
column68: The knowledge that if you set your mind to it, you can accomplish many things, even starting your own business and expanding your net worth.
column69: I want the opportunity to give back ~ my mentor and coach is the most amazing human I know and I want to pay it forward.
column70: Nursing is hard and as we have learned over the past three years, it is a profession that has lost its vision. However, there are so many things that one can do as a nurse, especially advocate for each other. There are many different areas of nursing pathways that one can take and that is an advantage of nurses that this profession has. Communication and advocacy of patients directly influences nursing and has allowed me to speak up in a manner I never thought I could before, not just for myself or my patients, but also for the nursing profession. Nurses are influencers both in the boardroom and in government.
column71: I was bullied and I was teased because I had dark skin and I spoke with an accent. My parents were first generation from Puerto Rico, we spoke Spanish at home, and we were raised differently from those who we lived by. I witnessed how my mom and dad were treated depending on where they were. It was very difficult and it made me want to ensure that I or my family members would not be treated any differently.

column67: I think it would be an amazing experience to inspire and excite others that anything is possible when you set your mind to it. I have challenged myself throughout my life to be better and do better. I have accomplished many incredible feats that I still have to pinch myself to check that this is now my reality! I have my doctorate in a profession, Nursing, that I truly love. I am lucky enough to be involved in Women's Services and I started my own consulting company, building my intellectual property. There are so many opportunities that I have been provided and although it took me a minute to get myself headed in the right direction, I made it! #orgullosadeserlatina #befearless #nursesrock
column68: The knowledge that if you set your mind to it, you can accomplish many things, even starting your own business and expanding your net worth.
column69: I want the opportunity to give back ~ my mentor and coach is the most amazing human I know and I want to pay it forward.
column70: Nursing is hard and as we have learned over the past three years, it is a profession that has lost its vision. However, there are so many things that one can do as a nurse, especially advocate for each other. There are many different areas of nursing pathways that one can take and that is an advantage of nurses that this profession has. Communication and advocacy of patients directly influences nursing and has allowed me to speak up in a manner I never thought I could before, not just for myself or my patients, but also for the nursing profession. Nurses are influencers both in the boardroom and in government.
column71: I was bullied and I was teased because I had dark skin and I spoke with an accent. My parents were first generation from Puerto Rico, we spoke Spanish at home, and we were raised differently from those who we lived by. I witnessed how my mom and dad were treated depending on where they were. It was very difficult and it made me want to ensure that I or my family members would not be treated any differently.

She was undocumented growing up and want to help students in a similare situation. She prefers Korean students if possible, but will work with whoever. 10/12/22 SB
column66: Jenny Park (She/Her) serves as the inaugural Equity and Inclusion Specialist of the Office of Equity and Inclusion at the University of Massachusetts Global. Jenny describes herself as ?Korean and almost American.? Being formerly undocumented has changed the way she views immigration and legality in the United States. She has been involved in many legislative visits to Congressmembers? offices and rallies to pass immigration policies. Through the process, she realized the key to being heard as a minority and seen was to educate people. Her interest in education is to incorporate inclusion of silenced and invisible minorities and empowerment into the curriculum. Jenny hopes to continue her life-long journey to creating equitable, diverse, and inclusive space in education. She also believes in amplifying lived experiences and incorporating them in the education system. To practice what she preaches, her thesis project is titled, ?Examining Asian American Curricula Through the Lens of Critical Theory and Historically Responsive Literature Framework.? Jenny holds a Masters of Education for the Social and Cultural Analysis of Education program from California State University, Long Beach and a Bachelor of Art in Psychology from University of California, Irvine.
column67: As a first-generation Asian American undocumented immigrant myself, I want to support fellow immigrant students who might experience and face hardships as immigrants in the U.S. Key to thriving as immigrants in the U.S. is access to resources, community and resilience. I would like to continue to support my immigrant community and return the love and support I once had.
column68: I would like to share interview skills, professional development opportunities, pathways for becoming an undocumented professional, etc.
column69: I would like to gain touch with the community. I am now a U.S. citizen but I still want to be able to advocate and support the immigrant community. I want to maintain my roots from this experience.
column70: I believe interns can learn strategies on negotiating for what you need as a human being in the work place , organizational tips, and navigating different cultures in the U.S.
column71: I was faced with status issues and the unforeseeable future in terms of career. I fortunately had DACA but I was always afraid of deportation and limitations to my potential due to my immigration status.
column72: I was part of a non-profit organization called Korean Resource Center (KRC). It was an immigrant-rights focused organization. Back in 2017, when Trump was initiated to rescind DACA, I went with KRC to Washington D.C. to protest behind the White House for 10 days. From there, I began meeting with legislatures on the importance of immigration reform and how it impacts our daily lives. I also became involved in making sure U.S. citizens vote. So, with KRC, I visited college campuses to help students register to boost young voters.
column78: Social and Cultural Analysis of Education, 2021

She moved from Russia with just her Mom when she was seven and moved around the world before settling in the US in high school. I fell into recruiting as a career path by chance but through hard work and pursuit of challenging opportunities I landed into my current role at Meta (formerly Facebook) where I currently work as Functional Analytics Business Partner. column196: All of the companies I hire for are in the finance industry. My responsibilities include sourcing high quality candidates through LinkedIn/Indeed/ZipRecruiter,  and my companies internal ATS. After sourcing I reach out and determine what their career goals are and see which one of my open jobs they would be interested in. I then help them edit their resumes and submit them to jobs they are interested in and continue to submit them to any new jobs that come out that they like. When offered interviews with my clients, I conduct a thorough prep call at least 24 hours before hand to ensure they are prepared for the interview. I continue to submit, prep, and generally check in with candidates until they are offered a job which they accept. At that point my companies onboarding team takes over.

column63:
Yann seems like a very busy professional who likes to keep meetings rather short than long. He might come off a little bit rude but I think it?s just him being busy and coming from a different culture. He grew up in France, studied and got some work experience. He moved to the UK to study and work and then moved to Italy and Africa while working for the UN. Afterwards he moved to the US in 2013 and started his Real Estate Career. He?s been in 6 different countries throughout his career. He can personally relate to immigrant students coming to the US and having to adjust to a whole new culture and the challenges that come with it. He would like to provide guidance to interns and teach them new career skills. He likes that this program has mostly students being immigrants or coming from an immigrant background. He?s fluent in French and proficient in Spanish. 

Important to know: He?s interested in Seminars only as of right now and would let us know if that changes. He?s currently too busy to participate in the FY but would like to give back as a Seminar Speaker. He provided me with a list of topics he feels comfortable talking about in Seminars after I shared the Seminar topics with him: Communication - All about emailing!, Communication - Present your best self!, Professionalism - At the workplace, Leadership - Where are you headed? Vision and Goal-Setting, Leadership - Essentials (Integrity, Empathy, Authenticity, Emotional Intelligence, Humility). column66: I was born and raised in Queens NY, one of three boys. My father owned a gas station, then an auto repair and tow business where my brothers and I all worked from very early on. In 2004 I was transferred to the Major Case unit and promoted to Detective 2nd grade. In 2009 was assigned to the FBI?s Safe Streets Task Force as a task force officer. In 2015 I was promoted to Detective 1st grade. I retired from the NYPD in 2019, after 30 years of service. In 2021 I was hired by the FBI as a community outreach specialist where I currently work. I am married with 3 children, a boy and two girls. My wife Margaret is a registered nurse. We live in Putnam county New York.

column66:
"Andres Rubiano is a first-year M.A. student at Teachers College in the Education Policy Program with a specialization in Law and Education.

Originally from Bogota, Colombia, Andres?s parents moved to Tampa, Florida when he was a little over two years old in pursuit of better opportunities. The role of public education in Andres?s development paired with the reality of how it had failed too many of his peers compelled him to pursue the work toward educational equity for all. Andres?s undergraduate experience consisted of work with justice involved youth through Esperanza NY, food insecurity through Hunger Free America, and working toward closing the achievement gap at Jumpstart. Andres has spent the past three years at Practice Makes Perfect, an organization that partners with low-income schools to help narrow the achievement gap. In 2020, Andres led the inaugural partnership with United Community Schools, the largest community school network in New York City operated by the United Federation of Teachers. After his time working in education, Andres joined Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez re-election campaign in 2022. Most recently, Andres worked as an Experience Manager at EY- Parthenon before moving on to be a Lead Facilitator for SuitUp while finishing his Masters program."

column72: Healthcare, Healing Through the arts https://htta.org/ "We provide information to encourage and support healing for individuals, caregivers, and their families. For healthcare, we provide free art to inspire hope and calm. For self care, we provide resources for creative expression that can help inspire a hopeful attitude, important for healing. We don?t plan for cancer, sickness, sadness, or injuries. Often these experiences catch us by surprise and can dramatically change our lives. True healing is not waiting for time to heal us." We create a juried art show each year, with artwork donated by high school and college students, created in the themes of hope and healing. In the past 3 years especially, we have noticed in the students artist statements that they felt less stressed/ depressed/ helpless and more uplifted when they were creating these pieces of art for the hospitals. We also facilitated the donation of some wood for first year furniture students to make benches to increase accessibility in local art museums. The professor we coordinated with wanted the students to do a community based project so they were learning to think beyond themselves.

column66: My name is Bindu Krishna, I am currently an EVP, Client Services Directory at Havas Gemini/Havas Health and You. Havas is the largest global communications company in the world. I focus on US pharmaceutical marketing and advertising, particularly access and reimbursement which is essentially making sure products are as easy and cheap as possible for patients to get. Before I worked in marketing, I was a consultant in two different organizations, worked also at a health insurance company, and also worked at a start up right after college. In college I was pre-med with a Psychology Major and an English minor. I also have a Masters in Public Health. I'm originally from the East Coast (NY/NJ), but now live in San Francisco, though I travel often for work. I am a first generation American/second generation immigrant, with my parents being from India.
column67: As I've evolved into advanced leadership roles the last several years, I've reflected on the limited representation I had growing up in terms of opportunities for Indians in "non-traditional" fields, and the dearth of leadership of Indians when they were in those fields. I had to navigate my way through my career through hard learnings and creating my own advocates. Oftentimes, shifting away from what I had learned culturally to be more of what an appropriate leader was perceive to be, particularly as a female woman of color. I had often wished I had access to individuals who could have guided me or provided me with their experiences for my own insight and growth. I'd like to know that my continued leadership and challenges can be paid off by teaching those who may come after to me to become even better leaders. I feel encouraged that there are many more opportunities for younger people today to learn from those of us who have come before them, and who are still actively working, to help support them in an active and encouraging network.
column68: -Building advocates, as well as advocate for yourself -Navigating through the implicit biases that sometimes identities (gender, race, etc.) generate, however that these identities can also be used productively for inclusion - Presentation skills - Effectively communicating towards a desired outcome - People management (upward and downward) - Client management - Self promotion in a productive way
column69: -Learning more about myself through reflection -Knowledge that a generation of leaders may benefit from my experiences -Teaching and instructional skills through the sessions -Long-term contacts and relationships (if appropriate)
column70: - PowerPoint presentation creation - Basic excel - How to run meetings - Planning - short term and long term - Client management - particularly in difficult situations - General resource and operations management - Team member management - Self-improvement - US Healthcare landscape (and some ex-US), including pharmaceutical drug development, pricing, contracting, etc. - How to be a high functioning team member/manage a high functioning team
column71: - Lack of true career path - Learning the importance of advocacy and friendship vs only competence - Being a direct female was a hard road to travel, particularly as I advanced in my career - Had a greater level of education than many others, which was both a blessing and a curse - I'm naturally introverted in a very visible career, requiring me to become much more extroverted - Creating boundaries to avoid burnout, which involved being confident enough in my value to communicate what I was or was not okay with

column66: Saima Malik is the Vice President of Human Resources and Recruiting at Avantus Federal. She brings 16 years of human resources experience within the private sector. As the Vice President of Human Resources and Recruiting, Saima is responsible for driving the strategic direction and management of the human resources function and capability. She also leads the efforts around attracting and retaining the firm?s innovative workforce. In addition, Saima serves at the Ethics and Compliance Officer for Avantus? Ethics and Compliance program. Throughout her career, Saima has had proven success in leading human resources capabilities to include benefits, employee relations, performance management, compliance, and recruiting. Prior to joining Avantus, one of her primary responsibilities was to assist small government contractors in effectively building HR strategies, and in some cases, building entire HR departments from the ground up. Over the last several years, Saima has strived to cultivate a philosophy in which HR is no longer simply a process or reactive solution to the company?s talent, but is instead a proactive strategic partner that assists the organization in achieving its goals. In doing so, a primary focus of her career has become the ability to provide organizations with business strategies that are both efficient and effective. Saima holds a Bachelor of Science in Global Business Management and is a member of the Society for Human Resource Management (SHRM). She is also a recipient of the HR Impact Award from Washington Business Journal ? Best Places to Work.

column66: Hi! My name is Jan and I am a Child Protection Specialist working for UNICEF in New York. I am from Canada and I received my Bachelor of Commerce from the University of Calgary, Canada and my Master of Public Health from Ecole des hautes études en santé publique (EHESP) in Paris, France. Whether you are trying to figure out what school to go to, what degree to apply to, what career you want, or how you can find happiness in your day to day, I can help! My background is how technology can help in humanitarian/development contexts but I have explored everything and can help you too! I am excited to meet you!

I also have good communication skills and can empathetically relate to female high school students who are trying to determine what they want to accomplish.

I cannot emphasize enough that I am very lucky and grateful for the opportunities I was given. But looking back, discrimination being a female was a constant obstacle and continues to be.

column66: Mohammed Baldiwala is an eternal student of life and advocate of positive change with a vision to awaken people to their infinite potential so they can build the life of their dreams. Mohammed honestly believes that his life is worth living if he can change other people?s lives with his ideas, writings, and books. He?s gifted with a vision that can inspire individuals to portray the image of who you are and what you want to achieve from the inside out. He is the author of the book ?Wisdom Worth Spreading.? A book loaded with wisdom which is the result of his relentless quest to find answers to life?s most difficult questions. After spending a decade of his life immersed in the superficiality of the corporate world, Mohammed decided to break free from the monotony so he could pave his way to a more authentic life full of purpose and truth. He is on a mission to empower, inspire, and awaken as many people as possible. An entrepreneurial spirit who is deeply dedicated to sharing his wisdom, he is also of the belief that we only have a limited amount of time on this beautiful planet and we should live that life we?re given to the fullest. Through sharing the wisdom he has acquired along his journey, he also feels driven to make this world a better place than when he first entered. When he isn?t inspiring others, Mohammed enjoys being in the great outdoors, exploring, learning new things, and reading inspiring books. Above all, he loves spending time with his precious newborn son and lovely wife.

column63: • Screening notes:Daniela is living in Spain. She was born in Mexico, but has lived in the US and in China. She speaks Spanish, English, Chinese (conversational), and is learning French. She got an undergrad degree in Biological Sciences & Visual Arts (can be matched in STEM and Arts). She has her MBA (business match). She describes her path as nontraditional and believes (which I believe to) that she will be a good Champion and example for students so that they can see success in non-traditional paths and that it is okay to change your mind.  I think she will be a good Champion.
column66: Hello! My name is Daniela and I grew up splitting my time between Mexico and the US. I've always had varied interests and in college (Rice University), I focused on both the sciences and fine arts. After graduation, I moved to China to work in edtech. Five years later, I decided to move to Madrid, Spain to pursue an MBA.
column67: Coming from an underrepresented group myself, I experienced firsthand the unique set of challenges and knowledge gaps that have a direct impact in our ability to succeed academically and professionally. If it hadn't been for mentoring organizations like the National Hispanic Institute, Questbridge, and DREAM, I wouldn't have had the opportunity to study at Rice and later work/live at places like Shanghai and Madrid. Mentors matter, not just because of the practical resources they may or may not be able to share, but in a less tangible way, they offer us the liberty to imagine different paths for ourselves.
column68: On a practical level, I have experience in writing/editing for various types of writing, including reflective and narrative writing, academic writing, marketing copy and corporate communications. In the edtech company I worked in I was also in charge of creating training materials for both students and employees, which could come in helpful for students interested in education or find themselves managing a new team.
column69: I hope to be of use. Moreover, I believe in the common saying that through teaching one solidifies one's own understanding.
column70: One of the most important skills I have learned throughout my career has been about relationship management and emotional intelligence. That, often more than content knowledge, will position you favorably particularly in new or challenging situations. I don't think this can be taught directly, but I do think practicing self-awareness (through reflective exercises)while intentionally exposing yourself to new ideas and experiences can enable you to be more open-minded and empathetic.
column71: I'm still in the middle of it! Assuming you know which direction you want to go in (for me, it's working for an impact-driven company), it's a straightforward next step to do the research about which roles are out there and what qualifications they're looking for. It also makes sense that you should work to fill in the gaps between the desired qualifications and yours (e.g. the role asks candidates to be proficient in the B Impact Assessment, you can familiarize yourself with it online). The hard part is the outward facing steps, a.k.a networking. This step, and the amount of time and effort it takes, cannot be understated. The trick is to make networking work for your own specific style, especially if you're building your network from scratch.
column72: So many! I like zooniverse.org, 7cups.com (mental health & wellness), Let's Do Something - dosomething.org, Amnesty Decoders (human rights) - decoders.amnesty.org, Stepping Stones (remote English teaching) - steppingstoneschina.org ..

column67: I grew up in an environment where I was always told I cannot dream or achieve anything other than getting married and taking care of the family. I had almost given up, it's because I was able to find a great mentor and through his guidance here I am not stopping at any cost. I was able to grow my skills, make better decisions and gain new perspectives on my life and career. I understand the importance of helping and giving back to society through little guidance to young minds. It is at this tender age we can build great leaders who can make or break a society. It will be my honor.

column63: Ahmed is a very nice young man with a lot of understanding of what young immigrants have to go through coming to the US. He was born and raised in Arizona until he was 9 years old. His family then moved to Dubai where he grew up and which he calls home. He came back to the US to attend college. He speaks English and Arabic fluently and some conversational Spanish. He has a bachelor in finance and is in his last semester at law school. Ahmed wants to help young people going through the same process of choosing the career they're interested in and helping them apply for colleges. He experienced how difficult it can be in the US when you?re not familiar with it.  I think he would make a great Champion with his background and knowledge. CT
column66: My name is Ahmed Imam. I'm a third-year student at Columbia Law School and will be graduating in 2022. After law school, I'll embark on a career in corporate law. Before law school, I studied Finance at Arizona State University and worked at Oracle for two years as a project manager. My parents immigrated to the U.S. from Sudan. I was born in Phoenix, AZ but my family has been based in Dubai, UAE since 2005. Looking forward to an awesome time with you!
column67: As a child of immigrants, I know how hard it is to navigate a terrain like the legal industry. Many - if not most - people at prestigious law schools like Columbia have lawyers in their families. I never had one and so my path to Columbia and my first year of law school were filled with a lot of struggles as I tried to figure out how to best position myself to be successful. I want to impart the know-how I've gained onto others in similar situations to myself growing up.
column68: Tips when applying for law school. What makes a good law student. What makes a good lawyer. The role of lawyers in society. Any other general questions mentees may have on the industry.
column69: I hope to give rather than gain. But hopefully, just the opportunity to smile at the fact that I helped someone avoid the mistakes I made on my path to law school.
column70: People skills, business writing skills, networking skills
column71: Getting a good mark on the LSAT, understanding the application process, and once in law school how to navigate the system.

column66: Lata Murti earned her Ph.D. in American Studies and Ethnicity from the University of Southern California and began working as a college professor of sociology for University of Massachusetts Global (formerly Brandman University) in 2011. She is currently Associate Professor of Sociology at UMass Global. When Dr. Murti is not teaching, she enjoys writing, editing, volunteering, and presenting on topics related to justice, equity, diversity, and inclusion. Her parents are originally from South India, but she was born in the United States and has lived in many different parts of the country, including upstate New York, rural Kansas, the Kansas City area, Los Angeles, and now California's Central Coast. Dr. Murti has studied in Costa Rica and speaks Spanish.
column67: I mentored a South Asian American high school student a year ago, for the local Poetry Out Loud competition, and really enjoyed it. I found the whole experience to be inspiring; and the student and I still keep in touch. In general, I enjoy teaching and mentoring youth. I feel that I learn as much, if not more, from them as they do from me.
column68: I hope to impart the knowledge that we all grow and change; and who we are today may not be who we are several years from now. And that is okay. We should be open to such change and growth. Indeed, what we find difficult today will likely not be so difficult several years from now, if we keep working to improve upon it. But we will experience new challenges in the future. That's growth and progress. And everyone experiences failure, struggle, and challenges. No one is born successful.
column69: The satisfaction of knowing that I have helped to make a positive difference in a young person's life and helped them to see that they are more capable than they had originally thought. I hope to show them that what they may consider (or have been told) are weaknesses or negative traits can actually be strengths or positive traits.
column70: Strong written and oral communication skills (in English); critical thinking and analysis; sociological perspective; presentation skills; cultural humility; editing; interviewing; teaching online
column71: most people not understanding my career nor the purpose of it; many people underestimating me because I am a petite woman of color who asks a lot of questions; not having many South Asian American, female role models in my career; becoming a mother and having my commitment to my career questioned because of it

column66: My name is Rachel Dao and I am a Physician Assistant from Bayside, Queens NY. I studied at Hofstra University in the accelerated, dual degree BS/MS Physician Assistant Program. I am a new graduate and I am starting a position as a Medicine PA at Northwell LIJ Medical Center. As a first generation American myself, I would love to offer help and advice to anyone interested in the medical field.
column67: Looking back to my own high school days, I can only imagine the immense confidence I would have had entering the medical track if I had a SPEAKHIRE Champion myself. As a first generation American, it was occasionally difficult for me. I had no personal mentor in healthcare to look up to, ask questions, or confide in when I needed help. Now, after completing my masters in physician assistant studies and beginning my new career, I would truly be pleased to give back to the incoming youth and provide help to anyone in similar circumstances as I was.
column68: The knowledge I'd hope to impart are my own personal experiences and medical knowledge I've gained thus far. This includes my clinical rotations, PA school education, and my experiences with job hunting in the current medical climate. I'd also like to provide general support to those interested in a career in medicine. Though it is taxing, it is passion and hard work that allows one to strive and succeed.
column69: From this experience, I'd appreciate the opportunity to enhance my leadership and communication skills. Although I'd be passing on some of my medical knowledge I've gained thus far, I believe matching with an intern and creating this new professional relationship will teach me something new as well.
column70: PAs are expected to have numerous soft skills but the ones that I personally would emphasize the most are flexibility, collaboration, good work-ethic, and communication. Some hard skills that are directly related to these are obtaining a history/completing a physical examination on a patient, medical procedures, and the ability to see numerous patients in a given amount of time. I believe I could provide general information of these hard skills to my intern.
column71: The education in PA school was taxing, as some may describe it as "drinking water from a firehose." Some daunting weeks included 3 exams and quizzes in between. Other obstacles included the COVID19 pandemic impact on our clinical rotations, our education, and also tending to the covid related patients.

column66: Dr. Adelina Longoria is a researcher, story teller, problem solver, creative and encourager. She?s a Houston native and currently resides in Austin, TX. Dr. Longoria is a graduate of the University of Houston where she earned a Bachelors of Science in Psychology, and a graduate of Oklahoma State University?s Experimental Psychology PhD program. Dr. Longoria started her career at Booz Allen Hamilton, a technology consulting firm in the Washington, DC metro area. She?s currently Director of User Experience Research at Whatfix, a Digital Adoption company. At Whatfix she oversees the user research function, helping uncover design problems and opportunities, and putting the human experience at the center of digital adoption. In her spare time, Dr. Longoria mentors young professionals and women, travels, spends time with her 11 nieces and nephews, sings mariachi music, does improv, and serves at Celebration Church in Austin, TX.

column66: Luna Martinez is a Bertha Justice Fellow at the Center for Constitutional Rights, where she works on defending the rights of Palestine solidarity advocates, challenging the unlawful detentions at Guantánamo, and supporting Indigenous and environmental justice movements. Before joining the Center for Constitutional Rights, Luna worked at Palestine Legal and Earthjustice. She also started a nonprofit in Zapatista communities in Chiapas, where her grandfather was a political prisoner. Luna was a first-generation college student and holds a B.A. from Prescott College and a J.D. from Berkeley Law with a Certificate in Public Interest & Social Justice. She lives with two truly cats, Chomsky and Intifada, and eats too much avocado.
column67: As someone who has lived away from my home country since adolescence, I am profoundly grateful for the many mentors who have helped me find my way throughout the years. Before, during, and after my academic degrees, I've been fortunate to have people who have guided me, supported me, encouraged me, and believed in me even when I did not believe in myself. Now that I work as an attorney represented some of the most marginalized populations-- Palestinians, immigrants, incarcerated people, Indigenous folks, and those held in Guantanamo-- I have seen time and time again how the legal system isolates and punishes those who come from underrepresented communities. I am invested in serving as a resource for youth and continuing to nurture a community of collective support as we work toward a more just society.
column68: As a staff attorney at a legal organization committed to fighting oppressive systems of power, I think I can be a resource for people who are interested in (i) pursuing an academic path; (ii) exploring the legal profession; and (iii) supporting social movements and communities under threat. As a queer, first-generation college graduate, Muslim, Latinx immigrant, I am acutely aware of how inaccessible these systems are. I have provided a lot of career and academic mentorship throughout the years and am committed to democratizing access to spaces that are often unavailable to people from minority communities, especially BIPOC, LGBTQ+, immigrant, and Muslim students.
column69: I hope to grow and learn as an advocate and an individual committed to progressive social change by nurturing relationships with youth from underrepresented backgrounds and empowering them in a co-learning experience.
column70: My nonprofit is invested in using litigation in creative ways to empower communities under risk, so in addition to litigation, reading and writing, research, and analytical skills, I work a lot on building coalitions and drafting strategic communications that move our communities in a more progressive direction. I have also helped folks to map out their career path by identifying opportunities for students with an interest in progressive politics and social change, which is a very niche space that's often hard to penetrate.
column71: I went to a very elite law school where most of my classmates came from generational wealth and class privilege, and I knew very little about the legal field and had no network to speak of in the field. Understanding what a career in progressive lawyering and public interest could look like was incredibly hard because law schools tend to push students to do corporate work, and I could not have gotten where I am today without the support and guidance of mentors who made me feel like I belonged and like I had something valuable to contribute.
column72: I have worked with a lot of organizations! Most of them are listed on my profile. I have been on Boards of Directors for the past decade and am invested in providing service to nonprofits. The main social causes I work on are environmental justice, Indigenous rights/ sovereignty, and Palestinian liberation. Some of the organizations I work with are the National Lawyers Guild (the largest US organization of progressive legal workers and lawyers) the NYU Islamic Center. I believe my whole resume consists of work with non-governmental organizations, so I don't think I'd be a great fit for people interested in a career in the private sector or a corporation.
column86: As someone who works representing people who are or have been incarcerated, I hope your organization does not close the door to these individuals, as they can be important mentors to people from marginalized communities who might come from similar backgrounds.

• 5/31/23 : Dr. Hankins was absent and had a sub so Mr. Gomez tried to help the sub get onto zoom. It did not work so he sent them to Mr.Z's classroom. As a result the Seminars started late. Wendy had trouble with the internet at the office causing her to lag. She tried other methods and as a result was late - but that worked given the class was late. Issues persisted, so I shared her presentation on my screen while she talked. She was great at engaging the class. Two minutes before the end of the class period the zoom shut down for all. So the participants were not able to see the end. The Speakers rejoined to check in. SB
  • 5/17/23 Wendy did really well! She had the students move closer so she could see and hear them. With the help of the PSL she had them participating and answering questions. Wendy did great! SB
  • Seminar Notes: Wendy was able to step in when confirmed Speaker had to drop. She made her presentation quickly. She engaged the class well and got them talking. Her presentation ended quicker than expected but she was really able to get them talking for the 'Let's Talk' section so that made up some time. If I were to have her present on this topic again, I would have her add a few more slides in case the class is not as talkative. Wendy agreed to do 2 Seminars, but when I brought up the second one she was confused - in future make sure she knows about both / sees both calendar invites. SB 3/3/22
  • Seminar Notes: Wendy's second seminar was like the first. She had the students engaged, asking them questions like where they are from. When one student was shy she made sure they were comfortable. She told the student she used to be shy as well and said "this is just a talk between friends." She really excelled in making the Seminar a discussion and not just a lecture. The students asked many questions. Despite interruptions from the school (an intercom announcement and students needing to leave for a Covid test), Wendy was able to keep herself and the students on task. The teacher was also really helpful and made sure the students were engaged; before the Seminar started she told the class, "I Invite you to come close so we can have more conversation with Wendy." The students did and as Wendy engaged them, more came closer to the camera. She again finished a bit early but largely was able to keep the discussion up with the students. SB 3/3/22
  • Seminar Notes: Wendy was great holding her presentation in Spanish. She involved students and it was a great discussion. She explained a lot about the topic and had a good presentation. Would recommend her for future seminars. CT 2/17/22

Esteban es un líder en recursos humanos y desarrollo del talento, que piensa que la actitud es el punto de partida del éxito de cualquier profesional.   • Written in collaboration with Esteban by Hetal 1/5/22: Esteban Proano comes from Quito, Ecuador but now resides in Lima, Peru. He has lived in every nation of Latin America. Esteban is a leader in human resources and talent development, who thinks that attitude is the starting point of success for any professional. Esteban coaches business professionals to positively develop their leadership and soft skills to get all leaders to achieve their business goals. column63: • Screening notes: John works in the non-profit space but could also be matched on arts/creative, business, and STEM (IT experience). His mother is from Germany and he speaks some German. John seems very passionate about non-profits. Also he understands that internships, which are often unpaid, are hard for people to get if they do not have the financial means. I think he will be a good Champion. SB
column66: My name is John Mix and I was born, raised and still live on Long Island NY. I graduated with a degree in Art & Design from Adelphi University, with the intention to go into Advertising, and though it was not the path I took it all came full circle and I ended up doing Marketing but in the nonprofit sector which holds more meaning for me. I have been working in the nonprofit sector for 30 years, starting as a ticket seller and tour guide at the Guggenheim museum and am now the Chief Marketing & Development Officer at Physicians for Human Rights. I have worked at a lot of different nonprofits doing a lot of different jobs and that has given me a broad and deep understanding of the sector and how to be successful in it.
column67: It seems like a real opportunity to give a few young people a leg up when entering the workforce and hopefully speed up their career goals
column68: Some of the skills (both hard and soft) that are rarely thought of as necessary or useful but that I have hard learned over 30 years in my sector.
column69: Teaching and having some fun
column70: Emotional intelligence, communication, data analytics, design fundamentals

Ping has been in the leadership roles of Silicon Valley OD Network, and currently a board member of Lehigh University Alumni Association.
column67: When I was young, there were some people helped me, so now I want to pay it forward.
column68: I have many years of working experience in Bio-pharmaceutical companies, I also have experiences in coaching, mentoring employees and youth, years of consulting experiences working with people for team work and professional development. Also I speak Mandarin and understand several dialects used by local people in China. However, I cannot commit for 10 months of time. I'm willing to try for 9 weeks, up to one hour per week, as I'm already extremely busy, and having too many things on my plate. In the earlier session, I entered "I do not accept" for the 10 months requirement, but seems it does not allow me to submit the application. I may have to check "I accept" to submit, but hoping that I will be given the flexibility to do the first 9-weeks, before I can decide if I'm able to go further. Otherwise, I may have to drop without trying.
column69: When helping others I feel happy. If the young people become successful with my help, I'll be proud of them.
column70: Interpersonal skills, communications across cultures, if they want bio-pharmaceutical career for the future, I can provide more details for them, and I can be a more general career coach for them.
column71: As a foreigner, I faced language barrier, culture barrier, race/gender/age discriminations. That was the trigger for me to start working on cross-cultural communications, and further on Organization Development.

column66: My name is Estrella Mestres. Born in New York and moved at the age of 11 to Peru where I grew up and studied Medicine. I decided to move to the united states in search of new job opportunities. I am currently a medical assistant at a private cardiology office in Manhattan.
column67: This is something new to me and I think it's a great learning opportunity being able to share and talk to younger adults about my experience.
column68: I hope to be able to share with younger adults what opportunities they might have if they were to pursue a medical career in their home country and then choose to come to the united states. I can share from my personal experience, the obstacles I have faced and what alternatives there are if the international graduate decided to change career paths when moving to the united states.
column69: I believe this can help me engage better with younger adults, and hopefully strengthen my communication skills.
column70: I work with patients every day, therefore empathy, communication, organization and patience are a few soft skills I'm able to share. When coming to the united states the different softwares or EMRs used for patient records was new to me and also the health care system in general is very different, these are some insights I can share.
column71: There were some economic obstacles, time management and lack of knowledge. I came to this country and didn't know what to expect. I didn't know where to apply for jobs. Everything was new and it took time for me to adapt without any guidance.
column72: When I was in school I've participated in Operation Smile for 2 years, helping guide patient flow so they can be effectively examined. column66: My name is April Carter and I like to refer to myself as a Technologist. I studied Business and Management Information Systems at Washington State University and I manage software releases for IBM Cloud. I am originally from California, but grew up around the US, and currently split my time between the US and London as a part of an exciting new role I'm taking on.
column67: I struggled with a number of things on my journey to where I am today. I grew up amongst a community of first generation kids, all struggling with similar scenarios without anyone to help them work through them, provide insight and additional adult perspective. Due to this, a lot of us grew up without knowing our power as future leaders, or even a basic understanding of how to become upwardly mobile emotionally, educationally, financially and professionally. My brother recently passed as a by product of many of the themes that are normalized within immigrant and first generation communities, which has given me a strong want to give back and also smoothen the path for a young person who may be wondering how to evolve forward.
column68: For anyone interested in Technology, I would be happy to share my perspectives on education, the journey to becoming a technology leader, different traits to work on such as public speaking, confidence, etc. I would also be open to sharing my knowledge in terms of the breadth of opportunity available within technology, outside of coding.
column69: As mentioned in my summary, I have has a very personal impact as a first gen American. It has left an emptiness that I think has to be filled by giving back and ensuring at least a few of the folks coming after me have a less trying time navigating upward mobility and how to achieve it.
column70: In my role I have to be a critical thinker, influential, and work with people across the world. Being able to read the room and assess your audience, differentiate between client facing communications vs executive or direct report communications, etc.
column71: I didn't know how to navigate going to college. In my home, there was a "college isn't for you, we can't afford it" and because I didn't know better, I made decisions to work full-time whilst going to college as pre-med student. I ended up taking me about 20 years to finish my education, which is completing this year. This has become a challenge in my career, as I am Senior Manager level without the education to back it up.
column185: Will follow up with a second professional ref.
