column63: • Screening notes: Jan is the child of two immigrants from Sri Lanka. She is friendly and very warm and welcoming. She has a winding career path that allows her to be matched in STEM, Public Sector / Non-profit, social sciences, and health sciences / medicine. This path also makes her a good Champion for Interns to talk to if they want to do more career exploration. I believe Jan will make an excellent Champion. SB
column66: Hi! My name is Jan and I am a Child Protection Specialist working for UNICEF in New York. I am from Canada and I received my Bachelor of Commerce from the University of Calgary, Canada and my Master of Public Health from Ecole des hautes études en santé publique (EHESP) in Paris, France. Whether you are trying to figure out what school to go to, what degree to apply to, what career you want, or how you can find happiness in your day to day, I can help! My background is how technology can help in humanitarian/development contexts but I have explored everything and can help you too! I am excited to meet you!
column67: I have recently supported other students (not in a formal program but by recommendation) who are interested in defining career paths and believe that I have a strong skill set in supporting students map out areas of their lives to determine how to best approach their interests and skills (especially those interested in humanitarian/development/NGO career paths, but am able to also support those interested in engineering/technology as well). I also have good communication skills and can empathetically relate to female high school students who are trying to determine what they want to accomplish.
column68: The ability to not get overwhelmed in decision making and to be able to practically problem solve career-decision-making issues and to empower female high school students to be true to themselves throughout the process.
column69: I hope to gain skills in observing how the student's best learn and explore their interests so I can learn how to adjust my communication style to be more impactful and helpful for the students.
column70: Negotiation and partnership building, Project Management skills, Product Management skills, Technical skills in using MS Office (Excel, PowerBI, SharePoint), Creating and designing a community map for problem solving and video editing
column71: I grew up in Calgary (in Alberta, Canada) a city which was formerly very Caucasian when I was growing up. As an ambitious multicultural female in an environment which was designed for a mostly-male/Caucasian, I learned that I was required to put in more hours and sacrifice personal happiness to inch my way up. I cannot emphasize enough that I am very lucky and grateful for the opportunities I was given. But looking back, discrimination being a female was a constant obstacle and continues to be.
column72: UN Volunteers (https://www.unv.org/)
column77: Ecole des hautes études en santé publique (EHESP)

As a recent recipient of DACA, Kerry-Ann has had to endure the obstacles and stigma that comes with being an undocumented immigrant. With her consistent work ethics Kerry-Ann did not let that stop her from thriving in the private and public sector. With a passion for academia and the arts Kerry-Ann is also a photographer, dancer and motivator of all things positive. Her greatest accomplishment thus far is raising 5 talented daughters with her husband, Tekil.
column67: As a young adult I feel that my life would have been more structured with a mentor. With more guidance, I would have had a better understanding of my gifts and talents and how to leverage them at a young age. I wish to help someone else to avoid the pitfalls of life with a little help/guidance. In addition, as a recipient of DACA I know the hardships immigrants face and I am living proof that anything is possible. No matter what I have faced, my outlook on life has always been to stay positive, do good to others and good will come back to you.

column66: Born and raised in Mexico City, Renata has over eight years of experience in the environmental sector, particularly on themes related to forests, climate change, human rights, gender, and landscapes. She currently works as Senior Program Officer at a nonprofit organization in Washington DC called Verra, supporting the product development of LandScale, a global sustainability assessment framework and online platform for landscapes. Before joining Verra, Renata worked at The Forests Dialogue as Senior Program Associate on the Land Tenure Reform and Land Use Dialogue initiatives. Previously, Renata was Climate Change Advisor to Mexico?s Ministry of Environment and Natural Resources (SEMARNAT), working on international environmental agreements. Before that, Renata was Project Manager in Policy Advocacy at the Mexican nonprofit organization Reforestamos Mexico, where she coordinated the Landscape Restoration Initiative for the Yucatan Peninsula under the Bonn Challenge. Renata holds a Master?s Degree in Environmental Management from the Yale School of Forestry and Environmental Studies. She specialized in Environmental Policy Analysis with a strong focus on sustainable landscape initiatives. Renata holds a B.A. in International Relations from the Tec de Monterrey in Mexico.

column66: Born and raised in Mexico City, Renata has over eight years of experience in the environmental sector, particularly on themes related to forests, climate change, human rights, gender, and landscapes. She currently works as Senior Program Officer at a nonprofit organization in Washington DC called Verra, supporting the product development of LandScale, a global sustainability assessment framework and online platform for landscapes. Before joining Verra, Renata worked at The Forests Dialogue as Senior Program Associate on the Land Tenure Reform and Land Use Dialogue initiatives. Previously, Renata was Climate Change Advisor to Mexico?s Ministry of Environment and Natural Resources (SEMARNAT), working on international environmental agreements. Before that, Renata was Project Manager in Policy Advocacy at the Mexican nonprofit organization Reforestamos Mexico, where she coordinated the Landscape Restoration Initiative for the Yucatan Peninsula under the Bonn Challenge. Renata holds a Master?s Degree in Environmental Management from the Yale School of Forestry and Environmental Studies. She specialized in Environmental Policy Analysis with a strong focus on sustainable landscape initiatives. Renata holds a B.A. in International Relations from the Tec de Monterrey in Mexico.

column66: Christina Broomes has been in the biopharmaceutical industry for over 18 years and is currently the Sr. Director, Manufacturing at Ultragenyx Pharmaceuticals, responsible Drug Product Operations at the first Ultragenyx Gene Therapy Manufacturing site in Bedford, MA. She joined Ultragenyx in 2018 as the Director of Contract Manufacturing, managing plasmid manufacture and supporting gene therapy production at CMOs. Christina previously worked at Alexion where she was the Sr. Manager of Clinical Manufacturing Management, overseeing external DS and DP manufacturing for both early and late clinical stage programs. Prior to Alexion, she spent 4 years at Sanofi Genzyme as the Manufacturing Sciences & Technology (MSAT) Manager where she led and managed the Focus Factory Support Team for the Biologics Manufacturing site, focused on providing technical and regulatory guidance on the manufacturing floor. She worked at Genentech for 7 years in a variety of Engineering roles, including Corporate Process Engineer on a green-field commercial Formulation/Fill/Finish plant startup and as a member of the company?s Operations Rotational Development Program; having started her career at Merck as a Manufacturing Engineer and Corning Incorporated as an Equipment Engineer. Christina obtained a BS in Mechanical Engineering and a MS in Manufacturing Systems Engineering from Lehigh University, and an MBA from Pepperdine University. In her free time, she enjoys spending time with her husband Peter, 6-year-old son Jamil and 10 year old daughter Jenna, and continues her journey training in Krav Maga towards her second degree black belt.
column67: About 18 months ago, when the horrible occurrence of George Floyd's death came to light, I found that I could no longer be silent of the struggles of people of color, like myself, in the work place. column66: My name is Divya Karki. I was born and raised in a tiny village in Nepal. I moved to New York when as a teenager. English is my third language. I speak Nepali, Hindi, and English. I am a social worker from Hunter College. If you are wondering, "did you always wanted to be a social worker?" the answer is YES. I grew up in a peaceful country, yet with many issues such as poverty, sexism, classism, and the caste system. These "issues" lead to many other problems like getting to go get an education, who gets to eat that day. I was privileged enough to move to America with my family. However, moving here at the age of 14 came with its own difficulties, and that is why I connect with teenagers and I am very passionate about working with them. Being an immigrant comes with a lot of shame and stigma, but it has also taught me to be empathic. I have mentored at the Eye to Eye program, a non-profit for middle school children with learning disabilities. "Eye to Eye partners with schools to deliver the only national after-school mentoring program that pairs middle school students with learning differences with similarly-identified specially trained high school and college mentors." I learned about my learning differences and worked together with my mentee to make the learning process more pleasant. I enjoy playing chess, spike ball, watching documentaries, and watching TED Talks. I try to hike and camp and travel as much as I can. I find food extremely fascinating therefore, I try to eat different cuisine. Which reminds me, I am binge watch food shows while I attempt to cook. My favorite foods are Korean BBQ, sushi, momo, biryani, mofongo, Indian streed food and too many to mention. I am looking forward to exchanging knowledge with you. High school was extremely difficult for me, but I also learned about my strength & uniqueness. Looking back, I am thankful for those experiences because I can better understand the challenges.
column67: I had a hard time when I came to NY from Nepal because I did not have any support therefore I wish to do that for others.
column68: college related, carrier related, social work, resume, job search
column69: knowledge about others
column70: Communication, empathy, listing, curiosity, Problem-solving, leadership, empathy, Computer, Writing Skills
column71: I switched my major from BA to Mental Health because I did not know what I wanted to do. I also have learning disability so it was difficult for me to even understand why I could not learn the way others learned. But of course, after I was failing my business classes I relaized that business is not for me and changed my major to Mental health because I knew my goal was always to help and support others. I also looked at being an south Asian immigrant with learning disability was my power that made me empathize with others who struggle and feel like they are not understood by others. Who quote on quote do not fit the space and we have to make our own space.

column63: • Screening Notes by SU: I believe Abigail would be a great champion because she was already a mentor for a similar program called big brother and big sister where she helped her little sister excel in her career choice and gave her advice for college. She is a physical therapy nurse who can help mentee learn different sectors in nursing. For instance, her occupation deals with exercises, stretching, equipment, and hands-on therapy to manage patients' pain, increase mobility, and prevent further pain and injury. As a registered nurse she was not aware of the field of physical therapy nurse position. I believe this is the perfect opportunity for someone to learn nursing can root out to different fields in the healthcare industry
column66: • I'm Abigail Mawhirter, a Registered Nurse from Waco, TX. I earned my BSN from Abilene Christian University and have since worked on an Inpatient Physical Rehabilitative Medicine Unit, where I get to live out my passion for helping others to heal and regain hope as they progress in their journeys of recovery. It is truly a blessing to work in such a fulfilling profession, especially one through which I can see God at work! In my off-time, I enjoy running, reading, being outdoors, and enjoying coffee dates with friends. I'm excited to serve as a SPEAKHIRE Champion and look forward to the opportunities that it provides!

• UPDATED BIO: Kady Sylla is Senior Advisor ? Africa at the King Baudouin Foundation United States (KBFUS). Kady oversees our Africa partnerships, network-building and programs. Before joining KBFUS, Kady managed grantee relationships, partnerships, strategy development and communications at the Issroff Family Foundation (IFF). At IFF, Kady designed and implemented leadership and organizational capacity strengthening programs, including IFF?s signature ?Collaborative Learning Initiative,? and also managed the Tristate Africa Funders Network, a peer learning vehicle. Before IFF, she gained experience managing international development and philanthropic grants, working with public and private institutions like USAID, Open Society Foundations (OSF), and UK?s Department for International Development (DFID). Her passion for social justice and commitment to social impact in Africa are driven by her and her family?s experience of being forced to leave their native Guinea (West Africa) as refugees for settlement in New York. Kady takes pride in her African heritage and is passionate about equity and inclusive community development. She often volunteers as an interpreter for Harlem?s West African communities navigating systems like New York City?s Department of Education (DOE), NYC Health + Hospitals, and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services. She is fluent in French and three West African languages (Fulani, Maninka, Susu). Kady received her master?s degree with honors at the University of Sussex?s Institute of Development Studies (IDS) and a bachelor?s degree (cum laude) at CUNY?s John Jay College of Criminal Justice.
  • Kady works as Foundation Coordinator at the Issroff Family Foundation, where she oversees operations, grantmaking strategies, partnerships, leadership, and organizational strengthening program for the foundation?s grantee-partners. Before joining IFF, Kady was a sustainable development consultant for international organizations like USAID, Open Society Foundations (OSF), and International Business and Technical Consultants, Inc. (IBTCI). She has eight years of experience as an international development professional with broad knowledge of program design for social justice, policy, research, data analysis, monitoring and evaluation, and stakeholder engagement. As a child, Kady and her family left her native Guinea (West Africa), settling in NYC as refugees, strongly influencing her interest in social justice. She received her MA (honors) at the University of Sussex?s Institute of Development Studies (IDS), where she also worked as a research analyst on countering violent extremism (CVE) & inclusive development with a focus on Kenya.

column66: Tara Shajan was born in India. She obtained her Bachelors in Nursing from Armed Forces Medical College in Pune and commissioned into the Army as Lieutenant and went on to serve the country for 4 years. Ms. Shajan migrated to America in 2004 and worked as RN. She graduated from Grand Canyon University with a Masters in Nursing Leadership and a MBA in Hospital Administration in 2017. In 2021, Ms. Shajan also received her honorary PhD in Humanitarian Conflict Response. Ms. Shajan currently works as Administrative Nursing Manager at Montefiore Medical Center and as an Assistant Director of Nursing at Northwell Health. Ms. Shajan has completed her term as President of Indian Nurses Association of New York (2019-20) and now serves as the chapter?s Advisory Chair (2021-22). She is currently the Treasurer for NAINA (2021-2022). While in Abu Dhabi, for six years, Ms. Shajan was a flight dispatcher for the 777 Boeing at the Abu Dhabi airport. She has found that her nursing and flying training have taught her the true meaning of humility and distinguishing one?s self as an extraordinary leader. Some of Ms. Shajan?s core values are maintaining a positive attitude, remaining courageous and focused, and always holding others and yourself accountable. A fun fact about Ms. Shajan is that she is a certified Private Pilot!

It was in this position that I realized the small business owners were being looked over and needed better personalized service. As a result, Doctor Klene was born. While not running the daily logistics of Doctor Klene, I'm passionate about fitness. I'm an active member of a bootcamp style gym, run half marathons, and compete in mud and obstacle races on a regular basis. column67: I was for many years a professor, specifically at Seattle University. column66: My name is Christian Espinoza, I went to Mater Dei High School and studied Business Entrepreneurship at Santiago Canyon College. I am from South Los Angeles County, and currently reside in Orange County. My goal is to mentor individuals that may have had family/financial struggles and have never had a goal in mind to reach. I will prepare you and work with you to find a goal, highlight/sharpen your strengths, but being a resource to share my story of my struggles in life and being able to work hard to accomplish goals. Not everyone that is successful, grew up wealthy or in good areas, or have had all the education provided, and my goal is to show you how you can succeed without having all the resources given to you. Can't wait to get started!
column67: This has been always important to me to not only give back to my community, but also have a platform to tell my story of my background of humble beginnings, especially financially throughout my life, and being able to work hard, stay focused, educate myself and completing the goal of becoming a Successful Entrepreneur. This provides me a way to give back to my family, children that are currently going through the same and don't have a direction to go and sharing that every goal that you set should not be for yourself, but for others. Faith is a big component in my life, and I would love to include that in my story to my mentees. There is a lot more to share, but I would rather share it while mentoring!
column68: I have been always able to empathize/sympathize with all walks of life, no matter the age of the individual. My goal is to keep hearing others stories, and increasing my knowledge of how to communicate with backgrounds that I haven't had the pleasure of hearing- and of course implementing my story and ways to assist/mentor children that truly need it and don't have a strong positive influence in their life.
column69: I wouldn't call it a gain for myself, but a gain for children that need the mentorship and being able to provide my insight on life, faith, family, and career.

column66: Divya Sharma is a Staff Privacy Engineer in the Search and Assistant Privacy Engineering team at Google. Her research interests lie at the intersection of privacy and security, explainability, applied machine learning and human?computer interaction. Prior to Google, Divya has worked at Thomson Reuters where she worked on the `Privacy by Design' initiative and as part of the Applied ML Research team. Divya completed her PhD at Carnegie Mellon University, specializing in data privacy and security. She is originally from Chandigarh, India. Outside of work, she is passionate about theatre/improv and triathlons.

• UPDATED BIO - Dr. Romina Tomé is an Early Childhood and Social Protection Specialist at the Inter-American Development Bank. Previously, she worked as an Economist n the International Development division at the American Institutes for Research. Her work and research focuses on family and child well-being, development policy, and impact evaluations. Romina holds a bachelors and a masters in economics from Universidad Nacional de La Plata, and a Phd in Public Policy from Duke University. She is the first in her family to complete college and a first generation immigrant. Born in a small town in Argentina, Romina moved to the US in 2012. Guided by caring mentors and role models throughout her education and career, Romina would like to join this tradition in guidance of others.

Her work and research focuses on family and child well-being, development policy, and impact evaluations. Romina holds a bachelors and a masters in economics from Universidad Nacional de La Plata, and a Phd in Public Policy from Duke University. She is the first in her family to complete college and a first generation immigrant. Born in a small town in Argentina, Romina moved to the US in 2012. Guided by caring mentors and role models throughout her education and career, Romina would like to join this tradition in guidance of others.
  • Main duties: Conduct impact evaluations and research studies in topics related to international development, design research strategy, conduct data analysis, write reports and academic papers, presentation of papers.
column67: I grew up in a family with a high school drop out mother and a father who only finished primary school. They knew only a little bit about college or graduate education, yet they encouraged me to study more and supported me in everything. But I needed more than that, and since high school, I have been guided by caring mentors and role models. They have been there to teach me how to write a cover letter for a job post, to help me improve my English pronunciation, and to show my the many career path that were available. Without them, I would not be where I am today. I would like to help others in that way too.
column68: Conducting research in social sciences and how math can be used to explore questions about child development, education, health, and gender issues, among others.

column70: Perseverance, how to receive feedback without taking it personal, how to navigate uncertainty (because we do not need to have everything figure out the whole time), project management skills, data analysis/analytical skills, presentation skills.
column71: 1) Not knowing enough of some fields: I always said that I ended up being an economist by chance. When I was about to finish high-school, I was not sure what I wanted to do. I decided to study business because a friend said to me that I could be good for that and that made sense to me (even though I was in the nature science track at school). After the first classes, I realized that I did not like it business as much but my economics class was my favorite. So, I changed my mayor to economics. 2) Having to work in my last years of college: I wanted to help my parents with the expenses and get experience, yet because of that I finished college a year later than my cohort. 3) Economics is a field dominated mostly by males: I had to learn how to navigate that and being heard in class or seminars. 4) Taking classes in another language: when I started my PhD I was not super fluent in English. Compared to my native speakers friends, writing reports took me longer, and I was drained after a 2 hours lecture. It took some time, but I got used to that.

• New Bio: My Name is Lena. I am 26 years old and currently work at FMC, an agricultural company, as a Senior Scrum Master. I started my professional career in healthcare but later transitioned into the informational technology field. I was born in Cameroon but I've been a resident of South Jersey for many years so I speak both French and English fluently. Outside of my career, I am the owner of a clothing brand called FX Lenella, I'm a professional fashion show coordinator as well as a community outreach coordinator and a mother to a 4 year old boy. I have an array of passions but fashion, outreach and caring for my family are top of my list.
  • My name is Lena Dandy I am 23 and currently pursuing a higher degree in nursing. I currently work at Cooper University Health as a Patient Navigator. I was born in Cameroon but raised in New Jersey. I speak both French and English fluently. I currently own my own llc that sells clothing online and we have been in business for 4 months.
column67: As an immigrant and a child of immigrant parents of Cameroon, I?ve personally come a long way from cultural, political, and social obstacles that seemed to stop me from progressing and establishing myself. I personally understand the confusion that any teenager experiences during adolescence and being from a different country has advantaged me by providing me with different angles to approach life with. I understand that it is not easy and there were times I had wished I hadn?t felt that I was the only one going through a similar situation. Being a mentor would allow me to share my journey with other immigrant teens in hopes that it will encourage them to keep pushing to make something of themselves too.
column68: The knowledge of continued courage. It?s difficult to assert yourself in a country that you may be part of but not native to because at times it seems that political adjustments may be out to keep you down. Continued courage is important in order to remember where you came from in order to strengthen who you are. Continued courage allows you to embrace your difference from those who surround you to show that in whatever career or lifestyle you aim to live, you will always bring a different outlook to the table.
column69: There is not much I wish to gain from his mentorship program except knowing that I am able to help a student who?s shoes I was in at one point in my life. Just the feeling of being there for someone and supporting them on their journey is all I wish to gain.
column70: Knowing how to balance social and cultural norms. It was a challenge for me as a teen and sometimes I was ashamed of being different from my classmates meanwhile I was being forced not to forget that I was different by my parents. It can be confusing and overwhelming. I?d like to implement the skill of knowing or at least managing both worlds and using it to their advantage.
column71: The biggest obstacle I faced while pursuing my career, was being the eldest child in an immigrant family in all honesty. I was not ?allowed? to move away to a four-year university because I was needed at home. I had to work, and help around the house as well as help take care of my younger siblings. I would have wished to attend a university because I am sure the many choices I could have taken to further my career would have been different compared to those I made while at two-year colleges. Even though I saved a lot of money at these colleges, I regret not going to a university to pursue my career and being part of medical programs and laboratory internships.

column66: Ayanna Lyons is a Rowan University Alumna who graduated in 2016 with a BA in Public Relations and a minor in Philosophy and Religion. column67: As someone who has worked as a Division Manager with Petey Greene Program, a non-profit that coordinates tutoring inside of correctional facilities, I know the effect and importance of measuring the impact of a program and how to implement those results. My degree in Public Relations has provided me with the skills of evaluation and my 3+ years with Petey Greene has given me the professional prowess to make sense of data and in turn create real results that impact the experience of students. I have also worked first hand in creating success plans for dozens of incarcerated students while they pursue their GED and prepare them for higher education. I?ve worked with dozens of incarcerated students post-release to transfer to institutions of higher-education in NJ, NY, and PA. I?ve assisted with the transition of students' academic plans post-release to ensure a smooth adjustment to higher education in both community-colleges and four-year institutions. I want to be a mentor because I understand the importance of using a strength-based approach to guidance for young people. I am extremely familiar with the responsibilities associated with the role of coaching, increasing student achievement, and tapping into students potential.

column67: I have a strong passion for making the world a better place. Coming from a low-income family and the first out of my family to attend and graduate from a University - I wanted to give back to organizations that foster the youth and provide the resources/opportunities that I was not provided growing up, due to my background. In high school, I volunteered in several youth organizations that spanned from organizing community events, tutoring elementary students, and mentoring after-school programs. Giving back became second nature to me, as I constantly found myself inspired by children that I worked with through witnessing their growth first hand. SPEAK is a perfect marriage of my parent's immigrant background, my passion for giving back to the youth, and the exact demographic that I would like to support. Flash forward to my senior year of University, I signed up for an Empowerment Program for grade school students in Japan as a Coordinator. Not only would I have the opportunity to travel to Japan and be completely exposed to the culture, but I would have the honor of facilitating a program that would allow young students to practice English, uncover their skills, embrace their weaknesses, and most importantly - voice what their dreams and aspirations are for the future. While this experience validated my passion for mentoring the youth, it also made me even more empathetic to other cultures and backgrounds. Today, I am a Digital Coach at a local non-profit where I am working to close the digital gap for senior citizens in low income communities. While this opportunity is very fulfilling, I want to continue pursuing opportunities that align to my passion of working with the youth, specifically with those with different cultures and backgrounds. I want to share my knowledge of being a daughter of an immigrant and how to navigate the new world that they are apart of. I am excited to learn more about SPEAK and to be inspired by the students that make this program special. Thank you for listening!
column68: As mentioned before, my parents are both immigrants from Vietnam. Growing up in America in a immigrant family had many challenges, such as, language barriers, cultural differences, and difficulty getting help for certain school topics. I have been through these challenges and can provide advice on how to navigate and adapt to the new world. I can provide a safe place for them to share their concerns, ask their questions, and provide resources/opportunities for them to grow.
column69: I want to foster our next generation of leaders. Immigrants have always been the backbone to this country and I would like to be apart of that journey for future generations. I also want to build confidence for young girls out there and show them that they are able to do what they want to do in this world. I want to make them feel that their dreams are tangible and they they have the support and tools to be able to get there.
column70: Resume building, Microsoft Word, Microsoft Excel, Google Suite (e.g Doc, Sheet).
column71: As my parents grew up in Vietnam, they did not have knowledge of how the American school system worked. They were not able to give me advice on how to build my experience to get into college or to obtain a job after graduation. I had to reach out to peers and conduct extensive research to understand how to get from point A to point B. If I had a mentor at a young age, I believe this obstacle would have been completely eliminated.
column72: Yes. My first experience was in high school during a after-school program. This helped improve my communication and public speaking skills at a young age. It also got me very excited about working with the youth since I was able to see growth very quickly through the activities that worked on together. My next experience was in Japan 2016, where I was a coordinator for several empowerment programs. Not only did I help facilitate the program, but I lived with host families with young girls that were apart of the program. During my time with the host family, I bonded with each of the girls and was with point of contact for any questions that they had. That experience was the most fulfilling for me because I saw the girl's confidence grow each week that we worked together. I also became exposed to the Japanese culture, which got me very excited to learn more about other cultures.

column67: I grew up with a sister who wanted nothing to do with me, and so, I felt alone. Of course, as the youngest of three I learn from my siblings mistakes, but I remember always feeling like I could be the best older sibling ever. When I got to middle school, I had one friend who was a year younger than me, and as we grew up and survived high school together, I always loved providing her with advice. Whether that was about boys, school, family drama, or really anything. As I entered college, I befriended more and more girls just a year younger than me. I think it's important to mention that I also worked internships all throughout college, which is part of the wisdom I had to offer. Mentoring and providing guidance has become part of who I am, and graduating college in quarantine has made me realize that I want to reengage that muscle and connect with new young people who might not be around people who can provide them the type of guidance I wish my sister had provided me.
column68: As a recent college graduate, someone who has held internships throughout college and worked in the career coaching world, I feel that I can provide mentees with insight into how to set yourself up for success before, during and after college. And if they cant afford college, reassure them that there are so many other pathways to success. I plan on showing them videos, doing exercises and providing them tools to help them realize their own potential. As I've gone through a lot personally, (parent separation, sibling with depression, failure, and a whole host of other things) I regularly give personal advice to my friends that often helps them cope with whatever is going on around them and stay focused on themselves.

column66: • Susan Philipose, MD completed an Emergency Medicine residency in Long Island Jewish Medical Center.  She has lived in Queens for most of her life, with a few notable sojourns away. After graduating from a specialized high school in NY, which sometimes featured a daily 4-hour commute, she went on to study Psychology at Hamilton College. While there, she learned how much richer life could be when she didn't take the obvious path to money or success, and also when the commute to class was just a 5-minute walk.  After college, she explored working in a few different industries, including test-prep, advertising and law. Her adventures and misadventures helped her formulate an idea of what she wanted out of her career. This led her to a dual-degree program with Tufts University School of Medicine and the Fletcher School of Law and Diplomacy, in Boston, MA, to become a physician with a focus on global development. Through these graduate programs and through her residency, she was able to work as a UN volunteer in Indonesia to help with  reconstruction after the 2005 tsunami, work in hospitals in Ghana and teach an Emergency Medicine curriculum to residents in India. She plans on working with communities in developing countries to help with healthcare delivery while she continues to serve those in her own Emergency Department.

I want to help inspire the younger generation to reach for the stars and to help them understand that there are no limitations to what they can achieve. As a first generation female, I understand sometimes things can get difficult. I've had to overcome self-esteem issues and push myself through difficult situations to prove myself. I want to be that support system to guide them and help them achieve their goals.

2020 response:
I see the SPEAK Mentorship program to be an opportunity to help girls who will have similar experiences to mine as a child of immigrant parents. I was fortunate to be the youngest of large family with a number of female role models who helped me navigate school and career choices as I progressed. I'd like to provide that same kind of guidance and support to others. I know high school to be a exciting but confusing time in a girl's life, a time in which having someone who has been through it can really help in setting the foundation
column68:
Should my mentee be interested in pharmacy, I hope that my broad range of pharmacy experience can help guide her towards a decision. Furthermore, my experience in school and in the workplace as a first generation female can help prepare my mentee for potential future obstacles and how I've worked through them. I'd like to be, above all, a support for her. I'd like to listen to what she wants from our sessions, what I can help her with, and draw from my experiences to help her in whatever capacity I can.

2020 response: 
Generally how to set yourself up for success as woman in the working world. Happy to help with college applications; identifying volunteer or other opportunities for personal and professional growth; how to learn about different industries and careers. But also skills such as goal setting, developing routine and discipline, improving personal performance and mindfulness
column69:

Using my personal experiences to help develop others; honing in on my own leadership skills as a young careerist; getting involved and giving back to a community I identify with
column70:
How to navigate pharmacy school including studying, work-school-life balance, social relationships, networking; areas of pharmacy - personal experience includes community, specialty and managed care pharmacy but I can speak to other fields as well and involve friends involved in other fields; interviewing techniques - I've been a manager and can help guide them in what employers seek; additional schooling decisions - having done my MBA, I understand that the road doesn't always stop at the Pharm.D. degree; clinical skills

2020 response:
Hard skills: Presentation building (PPT), Excel, writing and editing, basic analytics and data visualization Soft skills: targeted communication, problem solving, project and change management, time management and organization, public speaking
column71:
I'll never forget that during my first week of work as pharmacy manager of Walmart, I realized that I was the youngest person in the pharmacy. My staff didn't take me seriously initially; they thought I'd succumb to the pressure of the job and leave quickly. Because of this, I had to figure out how to be a leader and be taken seriously despite my age and gender. I'll also never forget - my staff pharmacist was an older male. A patient called once, wasn't happy with my answer to her complaint, and asked to speak with the pharmacy manager. When I told her I was the pharmacy manager, she scoffed and said "don't lie, you're a technician, the guy has to be the manager, you sound like a kid." Those around me always underestimate me because of my age and gender, both in the workplace with coworkers and with the people I serve. I've hit this issue many times but I will never forget those as my first experiences. It's when I realized that, no matter how progressive society says they are, there will always be people who don't have faith in you or will actively bring you down. It's given me the drive to work through those obstacles so I can get to where I'm meant to be - affecting change at a higher level and ensuring patients get what they're entitled to - safe and cost-effective care.

2020 response:
Pursuing a "non traditional" career in healthcare required me to do a lot of research on how to advance. Healthcare management was and still is a growing field, so there is no one way of pursing a particular path. For this, I had to learn about many different jobs in the field, understand my interests, values and skill set in order to leverage opportunities in which I would be successful. Jobs and internships weren't always paid, but I took them for the experience. Parts of my schooling curriculum weren't always easy, but I worked through them doing my best and learning to focus on the areas I could improve in.
column72:
Yes - St. John's University. I was a mentor to an incoming freshman. It was a rewarding experience and she said I eased her fears of entering a new school and a challenging doctorate program so she was able to approach it more confidently.

During high school and college I was a math and English tutor for students 5-16. I was also a peer mentor, essentially serving as ambassador for the school and also acting as a peer mediator in student disputes.

Some hard skills I can teach include writing for various audiences as well as report writing/academic writing, marketing/branding, graphic design, program management/planning, etc.
column71: As a first generation college student and a Black woman from a low-income household, I faced several obstacles on the path to pursuing my career interests. Many of my obstacles derived from being on a college campus and working two jobs to send money home, or working three jobs before entering graduate school to ensure I could enroll, attend, and graduate with little debt. Additionally, attending an Ivy League for graduate school was also tough because I often faced stereotype threat and felt as though I did not quite fit in. I did this all while working full time. These obstacles certainly taught me the importance of hard work and perseverance, but it also taught me the value of having a supportive community and leveraging your story to remain resilient.

column67: I want to become a SPEAK Mentorship mentor for so many reasons. First, I want to be a mentor because I want to provide efficient counsel and resources to young women that may be unavailable. I always want them to know and understand that there are women like them within their age group that's willing to advocate on their behalf for the sake of their well-being. I want to help them in every way possible to be the best version of themselves that they thought they can never be by coaching and preparing them for change in the world. Secondly, I want to be a mentor because I want to motivate young women to challenge their ideas, thoughts, and insights about their career and educational choices by overlooking their potential to be beyond what they may be familiar with or want to do. I want to amplify their visions by enhancing their thinking capabilities in a way that encourages them to validate opportunities that will greatly benefit them. Thirdly, I want to be a mentor because I get to use my everyday life experiences to advice and teach them on things that are significant to know and stay away from. I will share stories with them from a place of empathy, compassion, and understanding to show them that people go through many of the same circumstances in life without knowing or realizing it. I always want to use my actions, my care, my love, and my words towards these young women to show them that I believe in who they can be, I support them, and I trust that they will never fail in whatever they choose to do with their lives. Last of all, I want to be a mentor because I want to help women overall in ways that I have never had. I never had help to overcome many of the situations that I was enduring in my life because no one knew how to. The only true person that I know and thankful to believe in to help me with all things daily is God. And, I want to use my love for God, my love for the community of women, and my life as a platform to be a role model and change the lives of those women who desperately need and want it.
column68: Some of the knowledge that I hope to impart is to acknowledge conflict of interests in conversations that I may have with my mentees, build trust with them, give them encouragement by offering them sincere positive feedback, and managing risks by protecting my mentees from any harm.
column69: I want to gain more opportunities within mentorship such as a full-time salary position. I want to make this mentorship experience worthwhile permanently. I want to refer this company and job to others who are interested in doing mentorship work. I want to be the best mentor that I can and will be for all people.
column70: Some of the hard and soft skills that I have overall are effective interpersonal communication, teamwork, dependability, adaptability, conflict resolution, leadership, problem-solving, critical thinking, optimistic attitude, strong work ethic, computer technology with Microsoft Office and social media, and data analysis through research.
column71: Many of the obstacles that I faced in pursuing my career of interest were major lifetime periods of anxiety, depression, and stress, rejection from many companies due to inadequate fulfillment of requirements, affliction and persecution from my family that wanted me to pursue their dreams for me and not my own. Throughout my life, I suffered with anxiety, depression, and stress right after I graduated college in December of last year. I fell into a dark place in my life emotionally and mentally for a very long time because I was very indecisive about what I wanted to do within the workforce while my reality was a living hell. Around this time, I went through many heartbreaks from bad relationships. I lost way too many family members and friends in a matter of weeks to months. I was drinking and partying to avoid all that was happening around me and all that was going on within me. And as a result, it led me to become very suicidal. I tried to kill myself four times. However, I overcame this particular obstacle by turning my life over to God and using my life experiences and the mistakes I have made to help change the live of those who may potentially be going through the same things I was. It was hard for me to find the career of my interest because I do not have any work experience. Most of what I have done that's on my resume is just volunteering within the community. And because of this, one company after another rejected me from working there because I do not qualify. This really broke my heart for a while because I started to feel that I will never be able to have the job I want with little qualifications. But, I came to realize that I can start with something small, work my way up with more experience, and then get the job that I've been desiring to pursue. Lastly, I faced a lot of affliction and persecution from my family because they didn't believe in me as much as they said they did or as much as I wanted them to. They also wanted me to pursue their dreams for me and not my own. And since I went against their wishes, all I get from them is discouragement, negativity, and strife.
column72: Yes, I have. I was a mentor at my church called Logos Christian Fellowship. My duties were consisted of developing and facilitating Sunday school classes for 13 to 18 year olds, co-facilitating small group sessions for 25 teenagers by discussing interpersonal, health, financial, and religious problems monthly, creating workshops, seminars, or conferences to obtain program ideas, information, or resources, and interpreting religious education activities to the public through speaking and leading discussions. I loved it!!!

column66: • Dr. Lily Chu has a background in internal medicine, geriatric medicine, and public health research. Currently she is the Co-Vice-President of the International Association for Chronic Fatigue Syndrome/ Myalgic Encephalomyelitis (IACFS/ME) and serves as a Community Advisory Board member for the Stanford University ME/CFS Initiative. She has also advised federal agencies and collaborated with the US National Academy of Medicine. Lily arrived in the United States when she was 3 years old from Saigon, Vietnam and has been fortunate to have had excellent mentors throughout her career. They inspired her to volunteer for SPEAK as a mentor.
column67: Your organization attracted me because I myself am an immigrant who first came to the United States as a refugee from the Vietnam War when I was 3. I have been fortunate to have some wonderful teachers and mentors during my education and career, including even now as a middle-aged person. Without them, it is unlikely I would have reached many of my goals. The time commitment and the ability to mentor online also fits my health (I have a chronic illness) and schedule better than the framework of other similar programs.
column68: How to go about choosing a university, course of study, workplace, etc; how to apply for scholarships; how to effectively study; how to cope psychologically with obstacles and succeed; time management/ prioritization; communication skills: public speaking, writing, working with different people
column69: Become a better listener; improve my teaching skills; gain insight into how younger people experience life today
column70: The different health careers available (not just medicine), different workplaces available (e.g. private practice, corporate, non-profit, governmental, academic), and the pathways to getting to the position they desire. Issues related to medical research: coming with an idea, securing funding, presenting/ publishing the work, etc. How to teach and work with different types of people from different fields, levels of education, etc.
column71: Lack of knowledge about selecting a university, course of study; Poverty (I did not apply to many schools because I felt my parents would not be able to afford them); Lack of social connections (most of my colleagues came from well-educated families with doctors and researchers; I had to search out my own opportunities); Youth (I skipped high school and - after middle school - was consistently at least 4-5 years younger than my peers); Chronic illness/ disability; Lack of knowledge about the US in general (my family came to the US as refugees and my parents had to learn English); sometimes, being female and Asian-American meant I had to "fight" for credit on work, be more outspoken to be heard, or faced doubts from people about my abilities, especially as a leader (I once trained a male student a decade older than me; patients invariably thought *I* was the student; at one point, it happened enough that he gently started correcting patients; other times, people would think I was the admin assistant when I was the keynote speaker or chair for a meeting)
column72: Yes. I served informally in the past and now as the mentor for multiple medical/ science students and trainees. Formally, I was a mentor for the joint University of California, Berkeley - University of California, San Francisco MD/ MPH program before I started to become sick. I learned you have to meet people where they are and that there is no one-size-fits all approach that works. A lot of mentoring is not explicit: I found some students would start to use the same resources, questions, etc. I used from just observing me. Learning can also be two-way: several years ago, one student taught me the difference between gender and sex.

I currently work as an Application Operations Specialist at Great Place to Work (no, I'm serious that's the name of the company haha). column67: Growing as an only child with non-speaking English parents, the door to unlocking my potential could only be opened so wide. My parents' abilities to assist me through school and applying for college was limited. Besides my peers and teachers, I had no one else to guide me when it came to making the biggest decisions of my life. I want to become a mentor because I want to provide the knowledge I gained from my experiences to students who might not be aware of the myriad of resources and opportunities out there. I want to provide support in a way that students can feel confident in the decisions that they make. And to top it all off, I feel a sense of gratification knowing that I have assisted an individual in providing valuable insight whether it be personal advice or external resources.
column68: After the mentoring sessions, I hope that my mentee will have gained another perspective, more resources, and know that they always have my support. I hope that they will understand that nothing matters more than the confidence and comfortability in their decisions.
column69: I want to gain another perspective in life and develop better leadership and speaking skills.

column67: I'm a seasoned software engineer that's 100% self taught. I neither went to school for programming, nor took a single programming class and have been invited to speak at Google in Los Angeles, spoken at an international conference about Javascript, and understand how much work and drive it takes to be a self made person. I'm a strong believer in mentorship because when I first learned my craft, I had no one to look up to, and had to fumble around the internet trying to figure it out. While it was difficult, I had always wished for someone to help me out. I want to be that person for someone who's looking to start their career. I am a strong believer in paying it forward and giving people a chance to take their life into their own hands, figure out what they want to really do with their life, and then pursue it. Anyone can do anything, and I want to help as many people who are willing to listen to me to be as successful as possible, whether it's working up to a job you'll love to go to, or being able to find a creative outlet to express oneself.
column68: I hope that my mentee is able to learn more about themselves and solidify what they want in life. We'll sit down, figure out "what do you REALLY want?" and then figure out a plan to make that happen. I hope my mentee realizes that they really have the power to do accomplish whatever they want - how to plan out their own goals to achieve what they want. Start a business? Let's make a plan for it. Get into med school? Cool! Let's plan it out. Become a programmer? Awesome. Let's plan that out too.
column69: The satisfaction of helping another human being reach their full potential, whether they know what that potential is or isn't.

column193: https://www.stophateforprofit.org/ This is not widely known outside of the Advertising industry but Facebook is completely out of control in how it monetizes its user's data. My personal stance is that companies such as Facebook and Google, who makes 99% of its revenue from selling the data their users generate should be paying a percentage of that back to every individual user. The addictive nature of these platforms essentially creates a horrible system where people spend hours and hours farming data for Facebook for free (instead of putting efforts to building skills that they can monetize). People should be paid for being Facebook's data farm - this would go a long way toward helping solve the income inequity in America today.

column66: 1. My name is Jasmine and I'm from New York City. I graduated from New York University in 2017, majoring in Economics and minoring in Business Studies. Currently, I work at PepsiCo as part of their Public Policy and Government Affairs group. The projects I typically work relate to economic research, strategy initiatives, and business development. I enjoy going to art museums in my spare time.
column67: I want to become a SPEAK Mentorship mentor because I believe in SPEAK's mission and vision. The purpose of SPEAK's Mentorship program is to develop the social and cultural capital of young women of color from immigrant families to become leaders of the workforce, with the hope of a future with a more diverse and equitable workforce, one that includes women of color. As a woman of color, and as the daughter of first generation immigrants, this mission resonates deeply with me. My parents worked long hours at a labor intensive job, and growing up I did not have a support system to guide me through the process of applying college or navigating the complexities of pursuing a career as a women of color. I did not know how important building a network was either. It was extremely difficult. I want to be able to be there for someone who had a similar upbringing and experience, provide that support and guidance that I missed out on and open opportunities for them that I myself did not have. I want to help advance young women of color socially and economically, as I know the more young women that do advance, the more equitable the workforce will be.
column68: As a mentor, I hope to impart the necessary hard and soft skills that will set up my mentee for success. Hard skills include how to use excel for analysis and other technical tools, or on a broader level how to edit and format resumes and search for jobs. Soft skills include interview coaching and teaching how to build up their professional network. I want to impart my experiences working in mostly white, male dominated industries and and teach them how to navigate cultural complexities that might arise from working in such an enviornment. Additionally, I want to provide a safe space for them to talk about their difficulties they have faced or might face as a young women of color, and validate these experiences. This helps build confidence and surety overtime. Through this, I hope to empower them as well as teach them different way to cope or overcome with identity specific issues. I also hope to promote civic engagement, as that is one of the surest ways to broaden world views, achieve personal growth and grow one's network all while investing and uplifting communities.
column69: Though this mentorship experience, I want to gain the opportunity to impact a young girl's life and t have the satisfaction of knowing I made a difference. While I was never mentored formally, I do know that I could not have gotten to where I am today without any help from the relationships that I developed along the way. Mentoring also serves as an opportunity for self-growth and self-reflection. I can learn from my mentee, and while we might have similar backgrounds, no two people possess the same set of experience or the exact same set of opinions. I look forward to being challenged and growing from developing this meaningful relationship. Mentoring someone is also an opportunity to advance my own social network, and from this experience I could potentially gain an intern or full time employee, and this will be someone I can personally vouch for. I can then contribute to advancing young women of color in the workforce.
column70: The soft skills that I can impart to my mentee includes professional communication, both internally at their business and externally to stakeholders. I can teach them how to manage a project multiple projects with various levels of priorities. My job also includes a lot of cross-functional engagements, so I can teach my mentee how to work with and manage different types of people, including people with strong/difficult personalities. For hard skills, I can teach best practices for data analysis and best practices for desk research and literature review. I can can teach my mentee how to use Excel and Powerpoint. I can also teach my mentee beginner's Python and SQL, if interested.
column71: While my two roles seem very different, they're both centered around risk management for the business. Some obstacles I have faced included gender and race ceilings. When interviewing for these roles, the people I interviewed against were mostly white men. This was extremely discouraging because, while we were equally competent, my male counterparts would usually land the job. It was through these experiences that I learned cultivating a network is equally important for career advancement. Once I started doing that, the barrier to entry became much less difficult. On the job, some obstacles I would face are having project lead roles given to my male counterparts. I had to learn to advocate for myself and expand my network internally at my company in order to have the same amount of leadership opportunities and visibility that my male counterparts have.
column72: I served as a mentor for college students and summer interns at my previous place of employment, Citigroup, and for summer interns at my current place of employment PepsiCo. Mentorship programs at both places include formalized trainings. For Citigroup, I participated in Citi's Early ID program where they identify talented college sophomores from minority communities and streamline their hiring processes in order to build a more diverse workforce. I was paired with a sophomore that had a similar background as mine, and through the program I provided her insights into my role and day to day at Citi, my own experience recruiting as a college student, general career advice and interview coaching. It was a great experience and I feel personal satisfaction at having helped her receive an official offer from Citi after the program ended. I also mentored summer interns at Citi. I trained and helped them develop the technical skills needed for the job while also eliciting feedback on their summer internship to better shape the experience for them. It was the first time our department had summer interns so there were growing pains on both sides, but the end result was very rewarding as I was able to watch these interns grow and return to Citi. At PepsiCo, I am currently mentoring a summer intern virtually through a partnership program with Corporate Engagement and Diversity department. I was matched with an intern with similar background as mine. Through this program, I'm providing general career advice, information on PepsiCo culture, and how to land a return offer as well contributing to a sense of belonging. So far, I'm enjoying this experience and enjoying getting to know my mentee.

column66: • Vaidehi Gajjar is a South Carolina based writer, editor, and mental health advocate whose day job is in hospital administration. She is a staff writer and the editor of Health and Human Rights at Brown Girl Magazine and is heavily involved in South Asian mental health through various organizations such as WE ARE SAATH where she serves as Director of Creative Content & Design, the PFA Institute where she serves on the Board of Directors, mentoring high school and undergraduate students to engage in public speaking and civic engagement, as well as Circuit Sehat where she serves on the Board of Advisors, previously serving as the Vice President of Correspondence promoting health, wellness and safety on the South Asian dance circuit. Vaidehi has also been published on platforms such as the Washington Post and has been featured on HuffPost, Yahoo Lifestyle, The Mighty, and Well+Good. She has also worked with and been featured as part of Harvard affiliate, McLean Hospital?s Deconstructing Stigma campaign, which shares stories attached to mental health and illness, and was able to have her display featured at the Exploring Brain and Mental Health conference in Mumbai, Maharashtra as well in Boston?s Logan International Airport.
column67: I want to become a SPEAK mentor because I would like to give someone else a figure that I previously did not have in my life. When I began my career in journalism and mental health advocacy, there were no faces for me to look up to. I want to be that face and that voice in the lives of my mentees.
column68: One of the biggest things I want to impart as a mentor is the fact that the journey to getting to where a mentee would like to be, is not always going to be easy. I want them to know that often it's not taking on the biggest task possible that makes you the best in your field, but rather taking on small tasks and rising up through them and proving your worth.
column69: I would like to understand what the new and upcoming generations are looking to gain from our previous experiences, and learn how they plan on utilizing them and in turn how I, as a mentor can help my mentee understand the field better. I also want to use this as a personal opportunity to grow as an individual and professional within my own field.
column70: Compassion Honesty Strong Public Speaking Work Ethic Timeliness Professionalism
column71: As I previously mentioned, when I first started out, there were no faces that I could relate to in my field. This made it difficult for me to be able to think that I would be able to reach the heights I have now. There was also very little support from my community around me because many times, a supportive community is what produces a successful individual. In addition to all of this, I did not always know that this field was even an option for me as I was convinced that Medicine was the best path, very early on in life. The obstacle I currently face is that even now, my day job that I get a salary from is not in my field, and not as I want it. Most of my experience in my field has come from involvement in non-profits and volunteer positions. This of course is not the norm for everyone, but I think it is important to understand that often, being a part of your field will require sacrifice.
column72: I have served as a mentor before, specifically with the PFA Institute where our mission is to encourage high school and undergraduate students to participate in civic engagement and public speaking specifically. My experience has been wonderful in that the students that I have guided are genuinely passionate about the causes we are involved in and are so interested in becoming the best they can. I also learned from my mentees and able to improve my skills as a mentor, and better cater to those around me.

WE ARE SAATH is currently ongoing a transformation, therefore attached is the link to our Facebook page. (https://www.facebook.com/wearesaath/) Circuit Sehat: https://circuitsehat.org/

column67: I'd like to be a SPEAK Mentorship mentor because when I was younger, I wish I had more mentors in public health who were south asian women who cared about the work, not just the title and could guide me on how to approach it and provide concrete examples of public health jobs. I want to give advice to young women who are interested in public/global health, but don't know what it entails or feel pressure by their immigrant families to rather go straight into the medical field for the assured high income or prestige. I want young women who care about community health and social justice on some level to know that that translates into many different career options for them.

column68: That failure is okay and is a pathway to success. I think immigrant youth, and especially the DAUGHTERS of immigrant parents, place a lot of pressure on themselves to be perfect and fulfill their parents' dreams. They're rarely ever thinking about what they themselves desire but rather what would make their parents most happy. If the path is unreasonably difficult or even if it makes them super unhappy, they will just try to make it work. If they fail, they're super harsh with themselves. I want to help these young ladies realize their true happiness and pursue their OWN dreams, without fear of failure or rejection from their parents.

column67: As a first generation South Asian female student, I struggled with many things like moving multiple times and constantly having to make new friends (of all races and socio-economic status), high school, college applications, FAFSA, grades - you name it. My parents were a sturdy but not reliable resource for information, they asked me questions which other kids would in reverse ask their parents. I want to be a SPEAK mentor for the students who can?t ask their parents, who don?t know who to go to and what the ?options? are in life. I had to learn - and do - everything by myself when it came to academics and understanding what college applications and schools look for. I want to be a sounding board for the students who may not realize what is the best avenue for them - not only to provide guidance, but also to listen to what their concerns are about their future. I want to be an advocate - because I know I did not have one, and after working and graduating, I realize the importance of having people in your corner who will support and help you achieve your dreams when you feel like you have no one else that listens.
column68: It?s okay to make mistakes and use them as learning experiences, but it?s also important to have people in your corner who will help you move forward and have your back when things aren?t always going your way. I hope that these students have a person who reminds them to keep going and to build that resiliency because it?s not about failing, it?s about your attitude and mentality which helps you move forward.
column69: I want to gain the pleasure of knowing I am helping a young student navigate today?s society with tools and tips that she may not have received otherwise. I want people to learn from my mistakes, and the best way to do that is to be open and vocal about what worked, and most importantly, what didn?t work for me. Everyone?s experiences in life will be different, but if I can help someone avoid a negative experience because they have the knowledge, I will 100% always do that.
column70: As an auditor, you have to be analytical and open to questioning people and the status quo and always thinking about efficiency. I am naturally a curious person who generally always questions ?why? something is the way it is which helps me immensely in my role because I am consistently ?thinking outside of the box?. I can provide my mentee with real examples of what it means to be ?analytical? and ?efficient? and ?think outside the box? and how to challenge yourself not only in work, but in your every day life by doing things which may seem uncomfortable or outside of your safe zone.
column71: I worked two - three jobs simultaneously during my college years to pay for tuition per semester, which caused me to focus more on my work than my studies, causing my GPA to seriously suffer. Looking back, I wish someone would have told me how important your college GPA is when applying to the top companies + grad school / law school. Although I think that my real life work experience helped me in my job interviews and resume, I could not even apply to some companies / roles (where I think I would have done great), just because I didn?t meet the minimum GPA requirements. I felt very lost my sophomore / junior year because I originally started in the business school, but because I failed classes, I was kicked out and was forced to choose another major. I then chose to study IT, which I always enjoyed (my business major was geared towards data science). Whenever someone hears the word ?IT? they automatically think coding / development, but I was never interested in that (nor was I good at it). I had a difficult time explaining to people what I wanted to do with my life because I only saw jobs for develops / coders.

column67: As a female in engineering, I found that having a strong network of mentors & peers has allowed me to pursue opportunities such as graduate school & internships. As a SPEAK mentor, I hope that I can be a part of that network and inspire students to pursue STEM careers.
column68: As a mentor, I hope that I can provide students with knowledge about what the biotechnology industry & academic research labs are like. column69: Through this opportunity, I would like to strengthen my mentoring and teaching skills. These skills will be extremely helpful for me, as I?m interested in becoming a professor and have my own research lab. As a professor, I will be mentoring several people ? from postdoctoral researchers to high school students who are just getting started with lab work. As a SPEAK mentor, I hope that I can become familiar with ways to help students succeed and prepare them for a successful career. With these skills, I hope to become a professor who can mentor the next generation of scientists and researchers and create innovatative technologies for regenerating tissue.
column70: I believe that I can help students navigate internship opportunities in the biotech industry. This skill has been extremely helpful for me, as it allowed for me to learn about different biomedical research opportunities and helped me narrow my own research interests. I have worked in a research lab since I was 15, and have spent time at different types of research labs as well ? the National Institutes of Health (NIH), the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), University of Maryland (academic research labs), and the biotech industry. I have had extensive experience with working towards developing complex cell constructs, vaccine preservation and even polymer synthesis. Outside of the research skills that I've developed over the years, I?ve also had the opportunity to develop skills like public speaking and technical writing. I currently present my research work to many key collaborators in the tissue engineering and organ development field on a regular basis, and have had the opportunity to network with these individuals as well. By encouraging students to develop their communication skills, I hope that I can help students gain admission to their dream college, job or internship.
column71: I am the first member of my family to pursue bioengineering and post graduate education in the United States. As a result, in college, it was challenging for me to find mentors who would help me make informed decisions about graduate school, job opportunities and even internships. However, by conducting research as an undergraduate researcher, I was able to seek out mentors in bioengineering who guided me through some very challenging decisions such as my first job out of college and even the graduate school I chose to attend. I?m grateful that both of my research advisers in undergrad took the time to guide me through these decisions, and I hope that I can do that as well for the next generation of students as well.
column72: I served as a mentor for my honors program at the University of Maryland College Park (UMD). As an undergraduate, I was part of the Gemstone Honors Program, a 4 year living and learning program where students work in teams in order to complete an undergraduate thesis. I initially served as a peer mentor for freshmen in the program through the CONNECT Mentor Program. Due to my major and career interests, I was matched with students who were interested in pursuing medicine & STEM careers. I helped them to navigate undergraduate research opportunities on campus and provided them with guidance on what classes to take. I also answered their questions about the honors college and the campus, as many of them were not familiar with the engineering resources or pre-medical volunteer opportunities. Later, I served as an alumni mentor for my undergraduate honors program (Gemstone Honors Program, University of Maryland College Park). Due to my major and work experience, I worked with students who were primarily interested in pursuing engineering careers after graduation. I provided them with guidance on how to navigate research opportunities at UMD. I also answered their questions about the Gemstone Program and pointed them to resources that were helpful to me while I was at UMD as well. I found that my mentees responded positively to me sharing my experiences, as they were able to see that I had also faced the same types of challenges and learned how to address them as well.

column66: • I'm a ABC (American Born Chinese) from the BAC (Bay Area California). As a daughter of two Chinese immigrants who only completed high school,  I was always told that "college was the way out," but was left to figure out how to navigate college and the years after on my own. As a result, I've done a bit of exploring: I completed my B.A. in International Development Studies at Brown University, and worked at a variety of sectors including nonprofit, academic advising, marketing/brand strategy, and organizational change management consulting (where I currently am). It's my goal to help provide any guidance that my high-school self would've appreciated to those who are willing to learn and listen, and I'm excited to do this as a SPEAK mentor!
column67: I've been fortunate to have people who have supported me throughout my education and career, and I wouldn't be where I am today without them. It's my goal to pass this on and help lift others up, specifically young women who have the potential and may (or may not) have been told by society that they aren't capable of achieving success, however they define it.
column68: If my mentee hasn't already read the book "Brave, Not Perfect," I'd recommend her to read it and reinforce its message: careers are not the same structured, linear progressions that the American school system guides us through. In order to accomplish what we want, we need to be comfortable taking risks for what we care about, and failing. I hope to inspire other women to strive for courage rather than perfection, and give them the support they need to identify what form their boldness can take.
column69: As a first-generation American-born citizen and college graduate, I've had to figure out a path for myself without the knowledge and know-how from my parents; I want to be able to pass this knowledge on to others who are willing to ask questions, learn, and make a greater impact on society.
column70: I've learned that a large part of being successful in business (specifically consulting) is a mindset shift: being able to embrace ambiguity, adapt to changes quickly, and have confidence in what you're deciding.
