column67: In my personal and professional experience so far, I have seen the importance of having mentors and I want to be able to fill a role as a mentor for a young adult who may not have access to these individuals and need guidance on the next steps. When I reflect back to my teenage days, I had no idea about what I wanted to do professionally but I was lucky to have resources and was able to navigate this process. I want to share these learnings with young women who are not only navigating these decisions but also trying to understand where they fit in the workplace and society as women from immigrant or minority backgrounds.
column68: As a mentor, I would like to share knowledge on how to identify your passions and skills and find education and career opportunities that relate to this. I feel as though young women in particular underestimate their skills and especially lack confidence. Over age and my career, I have developed the confidence in my abilities and strengths and I want to make sure I help every mentee understand their strengths and embrace them. I have also worked in male-dominated environments for most of my career so I'd like to share my knowledge on how to manage these situations as a South Asian woman.
column69: I have struggled to find opportunities in New York where I can be more involved in the community particularly in organizations that work with women from immigrant/minority backgrounds. From this mentorship experience, I hope to gain the opportunity to be a bigger part of the broader New York community and connect with young adults who are at different stages of their journeys. Even in college, I was a mentor to a young girl from Guatemala and since then, I had not had the opportunity to do that but it's something I have always enjoyed. I believe mentorship is one of the most beneficial resources for young adults and also feel like I can learn a tremendous amount from being a mentor.

column71: As a woman working in a male-dominated environment in finance for more than four years, I constantly faced being left out of opportunities or activities because I was not a man. Even though I was able to navigate these situations, it was frustrating and something most women will encounter in the workplace regardless of their career stage or role.
column72: Yes, I served as a mentor in college with DC Schools Project in Washington, DC. column71: I wanted to go to Medical school or have a career in healthcare after graduating from college. Since I had moved from India after completing high school, I was unaware of the types of school and what all was needed to get into college. I started at a community college and made my way into a 4 year university. I had no guidance from family nor did have any friends at that time to guide me with the process. I started exploring various healthcare schools but did not really have anyone to ask questions or understand the process. I was unaware that I had to take PCAT to apply to a pharmacy school or MCAT for medical school. All this information seems pretty simple but coming from a different country I was completely unaware of. If I had a platform such as SPEAK or a mentor to help me through school it would have been much smoother.
column72: I have been a peer mentor for Genetics class in college (UT Austin). We got together with the professor on a biweekly basis and planned activities for the mentorship sessions. These sessions were held weekly, 2 hours long, for students who needed help with their grades, test preparation, concept review or any additional help outside of lecture. During Pharmacy School at Rutgers, I have been a mentor for Renal/Gastrointestinal Therapeutics class during which I helped fellow students to review their Exam grades, guided them with their studying technique and clarified concepts. I have also participated in "Shadow a Student Pharmacist" day during which students from high school who had interest in getting into pharmacy school after graduation attended Rutgers for a day, attended lectures and we helped them answer any questions they have about the curriculum/school etc.

column67: I came to this country at 3rd grade level I didn?t know how to speak English. My parents didn?t have money I stood out since I had dark hair dark tone skin and didn?t speak English. I was bullied a lot, I got left back bc my 3rd grade teacher didn?t take the time to help me but my 4th grade teacher did. I wrote my college essay about her. She helped me get through my struggles and I graduated w honors. However at the same time my father was physically and mentally abusive towards me my sister and my mom so I decided to rebel and meet wrong kind of people. I was sexually molested at age 6 from a family member at a sleepover in my country and the pattern continued when I came to America. I was molested from my cousin at sleepover?s at age 14. I was raped at age 16 in my own house from a guy I was seeing, he took my virginity. Though throughout all this I had one thing going for me, my grades. I kept all this abuse in since I was so ashamed but still managed to go away to college and get financial loans to stay in campus. I graduated in 4 years and I dated men who were physically and mentally abusive feeling worthless thinking I deserved it. However know at age 41 a mother of two boys on my own I am confident and strong enough to let them know that nobody should ever put them down or touch them or make them feel like they are worthless. I want them to seep the confidence and strength through my veins onto them. I want them to feel comfortable and protected and that they can come to me w anything.

I used to watch the after school specials and those for me were like therapy so I hope I can be of some help to these kids

column66:
• New Bio: My Name is Lena. I am 26 years old and currently work at FMC, an agricultural company, as a Senior Scrum Master. I started my professional career in healthcare but later transitioned into the informational technology field. I was born in Cameroon but I've been a resident of South Jersey for many years so I speak both French and English fluently. Outside of my career, I am the owner of a clothing brand called FX Lenella, I'm a professional fashion show coordinator as well as a community outreach coordinator and a mother to a 4 year old boy. I have an array of passions but fashion, outreach and caring for my family are top of my list.
  • My name is Lena Dandy, I am 25 and currently work at Capital One as an Agile Delivery Lead. I started my professional career in healthcare but recently transitioned into Informational Technology. 
     I was born in Cameroon but grew in New Jersey and eventually moved to the Greater Philadelphia area. My passions are fashion, community outreach, music and caring for my 3 year old son. I speak both French and English fluently. I currently own my own llc that sells clothing online and we have been in business for a little over 2 years.
column67: As an immigrant and a child of immigrant parents of Cameroon, I?ve personally come a long way from cultural, political, and social obstacles that seemed to stop me from progressing and establishing myself. I personally understand the confusion that any teenager experiences during adolescence and being from a different country has advantaged me by providing me with different angles to approach life with. I understand that it is not easy and there were times I had wished I hadn?t felt that I was the only one going through a similar situation. Being a mentor would allow me to share my journey with other immigrant teens in hopes that it will encourage them to keep pushing to make something of themselves too.
column68: The knowledge of continued courage. It?s difficult to assert yourself in a country that you may be part of but not native to because at times it seems that political adjustments may be out to keep you down. Continued courage is important in order to remember where you came from in order to strengthen who you are. Continued courage allows you to embrace your difference from those who surround you to show that in whatever career or lifestyle you aim to live, you will always bring a different outlook to the table.
column69: There is not much I wish to gain from his mentorship program except knowing that I am able to help a student who?s shoes I was in at one point in my life. Just the feeling of being there for someone and supporting them on their journey is all I wish to gain.
column70: Knowing how to balance social and cultural norms. It was a challenge for me as a teen and sometimes I was ashamed of being different from my classmates meanwhile I was being forced not to forget that I was different by my parents. It can be confusing and overwhelming. I?d like to implement the skill of knowing or at least managing both worlds and using it to their advantage.
column71: The biggest obstacle I faced while pursuing my career, was being the eldest child in an immigrant family in all honesty. I was not ?allowed? to move away to a four-year university because I was needed at home. I had to work, and help around the house as well as help take care of my younger siblings. I would have wished to attend a university because I am sure the many choices I could have taken to further my career would have been different compared to those I made while at two-year colleges. Even though I saved a lot of money at these colleges, I regret not going to a university to pursue my career and being part of medical programs and laboratory internships.

column70: I deal with administration and the business of healthcare, but have some clinical background as well. I have dealt a lot with understanding different people's ideas and thoughts, making sure everyone feels heard, it also teaches you a lot of being grateful for every day even if your situation is not the best. I work in Radiation Oncology, and we deal with lung cancer patients who are sick and need our help. It is a huge amount of teamwork and collaboration to get a job done. There is no one important person, and passion is of course something crucial to any profession you do, but especially health care. We do not directly interact with patients in administration, but addressing their needs in whatever way they ask us to, makes them feel safe in their care and treatment. I have experienced quite a lot with changing majors in college and also working now as a professional, which can translate to someone starting their life in the real world. I remember being in high school and not knowing what I wanted to do or where to go, and I can help to shape the path of whoever I am lucky enough to mentor.
column71: Being a young woman just graduated from college, it was difficult in trying to pave my path and do it without anyone's help. The healthcare field is also very competitive and can be difficult finding your place and what you want.

column67: I believe that my story of a woman with a self-made executive career can be motivating and empowering for young girls that might not have a lot of leverage at the beginning of their professional life. Just few highlights about myself. Born in a small industrial town 1,5 hour by train from Moscow. Parents divorced when I was 7 after 18 years of unhappy marriage with my father mentally (and sometimes physically) abusing and suppressing my mother. He didn?t support us (me) after the divorce and mom had to work full time. As of 7, I was in all after after-schools, summer camps, etc studying all by myself and helping mom with home. I was a bright kid but not a prodigy, so I had to study harder. I took on the 2nd evening English school at the age of 13 when all my friends were out dating. I studied practically 12 hours a day. I started to work full-time at 17 to pay my tuition, rent and food when I entered the Academy. I went to classes during the day and I worked at the hotel reception at night for 3 years. I slept in between? for 3 years. Long story short, here I am, corporate executive in the international company ? the only Russian that got into US corporate since the inception of the company 38 years ago. I have great life and most importantly ? I fully support my mother. I bought her a nice apartment by the sea. She has great living conditions and enjoys beautiful life, which I fully happily cover. Being able to provide a happy life for my mom was my purpose and still is. Bottom line, lots is possible and achievable in life when one has a purpose, positive attitude and isn?t afraid to explore her potential.
column68: How to be resilient and agile, how to adapt to changes, how to be positive and open to the world, how to focus on what?s important in achieving the purpose, how to believe in yourself and put the belief into action.

There were/are people in my life that gave me strength, shared their wisdom, gave me a chance, encouraged and supported me. They appeared in my life in those moments when I needed them the most and ?gave? to me. Now I would like to ?give back? to those girls that might need that word of encouragement, empowerment and support while they try to figure out their way. If my experience or my words can help in some way at least to one person ? it's the best way for me to express gratitude to all those people that were/are there for me when I needed them.

column67: Growing up as an only daughter of immigrant parents, not only did I find it difficult to connect with my peers, but I also found difficulty in running into people who would want to help me out in the specific industry I was interested in - fashion. The industry itself is led by individuals who have been grandfathered into the industry, making it a very insular community. I want to help an individual through the SPEAK Mentorship because I know what it was like to want to do something with my life but not even have the right questions to ask. column67: I grew up quite poor in South Carolina and didn't have a lot of support with regards to school or career when I was younger. My own parents never went to college and so simply did not have the knowledge to share with me. I was able to make it with scrappy resources but it was a struggle to understand how to approach the choices I had to make and what they would mean for my future. I paid for my own education completely and have lived independently since I was 17 because of family troubles. I also have struggled with anxiety and depression at times because of childhood trauma. I think society leads us to believe that mental health challenges mean that we are somehow defective but I believe that they can make us stronger and more effective adults if we seek appropriate support. I believe that talking about mental health will de-stigmatize it so that more people can have the help they need. I have a lot of empathy for kids whose homes are not the safest places and want to make the world easier for them. Even once I was more established in my career I continued to feel a huge lack of female mentorship in senior leadership positions and felt hungry to hear from more women about their work experience and how to navigate the business place. A couple of weeks ago I watched a presentation by Susan Tousi, SVP of Product Development at Illumina, and teared up because representation is really powerful and we need to see people like ourselves doing the things we want to do. That's a big part of what led me to find SPEAK Mentorship and apply.

column71: That's a long story. My mother was not supportive of me going to college, and after a long battle I dropped out my senior year. The last class of my senior year. I had majored in math and microbiology. I had originally wanted to get a PhD in epidemiology (I still may!) but for a while it was very tough. I got a job doing billing for an optometrist, and I found I could still have some activity in the medical field without being clinical and I thought it was interesting to be able to read patient's charts and "pretend" that I was that doctor. I did advance to the point where I needed a certification or a degree to earn more money--I had just left my abusive ex and I had a 2 year old. I sat down and studied for my CPC while he was napping or after he fell asleep. I completed a 6 month program in 3 months and passed the CPC and was employed by a Fortune #5 company within 9 months. Now my boss is not supportive of continuing education--she doesn't know I'm going back to school to get my bachelor's in health information management and get a RHIA credential, but on every other weekends and evenings I sit and I write papers all day to try and graduate in February 2019

Liz is currently the head of Instagram for The New York Times, where she leads a small team that works to translate The Times's news to more than 16 million Instagram followers. Previously, she worked as an editor at Condé Nast, Refinery29 and Mashable. Liz studied photojournalism and journalism management at The University of Missouri, where she got her undergraduate and Master's degrees. In her spare time, Liz enjoys traveling (she spent a year teaching English to high school students in Spain in 2019!), trying new restaurants, going to concerts (she might be the world's biggest Harry Styles fan) and hanging out with her rescue dog, Bleecker.

It helps me overcome my self-doubts as they arise (which is daily and normal for humans doing brave things). They've achieved quite a bit and have so much experience to impart. I treasure them and am actively cultivating relationships with several folks who I hope will become future mentors.

• New Bio: Greetings! My name is Addy, I moved to the U.S. from Mexico at age fourteen. column67: I appreciate that this organization focuses on immigrant girls. As an immigrant myself, I struggled to find role models with similar experiences in politics and law. It was at times, impossible. SPEAK Mentorship seeks to fill that gap by introducing girls to women with whom they can foster a relationship to ask questions they would find awkward talking with someone who isn't able to relate to their experiences. Immigrant children can sometimes feel they are in a gray area because they do not fit into any dominate group in the US. For example, there were times when African-Americans have commented that I'm not "Black" enough because I speak or dress differently, whereas my white classmates also had a limited but somewhat different understanding of what "Black" was in America. I had to navigate not fitting squarely into boxes and the challenges that have come with that. I've heard other African and Caribbean people make similar comments and I hope to be a resource to girls in that situation. Additionally, I've had to navigate the social aspects of eating different foods from other classmates, having different traditions, and growing up with a stronger emphasis on religion than many of my friends. I believe these issues are common among immigrant children.
column68: I hope to be a resource and sounding board to girls who are trying to figure themselves out while they navigating two worlds. I hope to have girls understand that there is not only beauty but strength in their individual stories and experiences. I want to help girls identify and capitalize on their strengths, even when they are surrounded by reasons to doubt themselves. Where possible, I hope to help open doors for girls, so that they have exposure to career paths they may not have considered.
column69: My brief interaction with the SPEAK girls exposed me to how brilliant and accomplished these girls are. column66:
• NEW BIO AS OF 11/7/21: "With two parents who worked and lived internationally, my community growing up was both from and in a wide variety of countries. I lived with families from Bangladesh, Mexico, Sri Lanka, and Peru. Through these relationships and the opportunities I had to travel with my parents, I began to get inspired to work on some of the world?s biggest challenges like climate change and deforestation: issues that affected everyone but were particularly hard on the world?s most vulnerable people. Today, the focus of my career is about how to make sure the food we are eating is not grown in a way that hurts the environment and the people who grow it. I have worked for environmental non-profit organizations in Washington DC, Peru, India, and Guatemala, as well as for the Peruvian Ministry of Environment. My current position with Rainforest Alliance is to work with farmers, food companies (like Nestle and Walmart) and the governments in Peru, Guatemala, Mexico and Colombia to help farmers use better methods to grow foods like chocolate, bananas, and coffee that create higher quality products so the farmers get a better price for what they grow while also using methods that protect forests that are important habitat for endangered animals."
  • OLD BIO: With two parents who worked and lived internationally, my community growing up was both from and in a wide variety of countries. I lived with families from Bangladesh, Mexico, Sri Lanka, and Peru. Through these relationships and the opportunities I had to travel with my parents, I began to get inspired to work on some of the world?s biggest challenges like climate change and deforestation: issues that affected everyone but were particularly hard on the world?s most vulnerable people. I try to use my multicultural upbringing to support marginalized or vulnerable groups around the world that are trying to address climate change and deforestation. Today, the focus of my career is to improve both government policies and commercial systems to confront climate change and deforestation by focusing on and empowering marginalized groups impacted by these issues. I have worked for environmental non-profit organizations in Washington DC, Peru, India, and Guatemala, as well as for the Peruvian Ministry of Environment. My current position with Verified Carbon Standard is to develop a new framework that helps governments and companies understand and confront sustainability risks and challenges in regions where they are producing or buying agricultural goods (such as the risk of global crops like coffee and cocoa causing deforestation as companies and farmers expand production by cutting down forests to create new agricultural fields).
column67: I grew up living in the same house as immigrants from Peru, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, and Jamaica. It is inspiring to see how the resilience and adaptability that many immigrants learn can help them become better leaders and change-makers as adults. In my own life, women who took the time to mentor me has been incredibly impactful, and I would love the opportunity to play that same role for a young woman. While I have long been involved in women's rights issues, I have doing this work with a systems focus, and I have been craving the opportunity to volunteer my time in a more personal way. Being a mentor would give me that chance to connect with women looking for guidance in a more personal way, in order to then go onto have their own impact on the world. Most of my career has focused on empowering other people to make the change they want to see in the world: whether it's an indigenous community or a local political party. By helping mentor young women, I would get to do this on a personal level, and help these young women become their own changemakers - however they decide to do that.
column68: I have not taken a traditional career path, but often have created the positions that I later went on to take with organizations that did work I admire. This has taught me how to effectively network, negotiate, and to follow an unconventional path even when it may not have been clear. I am often the go-to person amongst my friends for anyone facing a professional dilemma or needing advice. Additionally, I grew up in a majority immigrant community, and have lived abroad multiple times (often alone). This required that I learn how to be self-reliant, adaptable, and tackle challenges I didn't know I would face. Ultimately, I think I could work with a young woman to encourage her to find what she was passionate about, identify her dreams, and then figure out a way to actually get that. I often work on emotionally challenging issues, in a space that often feels hopeless or dispiriting. As such, in additional to my professional development, I have had to learn about my own emotional needs, and what it takes to create space to acknowledge pain I face while being grateful for what I have, and using those as a basis to go out into the world and be an advocate.
column69: As I mentioned, I have long been an activist on women's rights issues, but in recent year this activism has been more impersonal in the form of policy advocacy or political volunteering. Mostly, I would like the chance to put my energies into a program that seeks to empower women not just ideologically but in a direct, and practical way. When I think about how I could have an impact on the world, helping the next generation of women reach their full potential so they can go on to have their mark on the world would be a fulfilling way to do so. And would have more impact than me working alone ever would.
column70: Time management and organizational skills, conversations with people from different cultures, finding consensus from people who may disagree, negotiation skills, strategic planning, public speaking and giving presentations
column71: I graduated in the 2008 economic crisis, and as such was unemployed for over a year. In that time I did some short-term jobs, but it took a long time for me to finally find a job that I was interested in doing. That period of unemployment was awful but I learned so much about how to be resilient and hustle to achieve what I wanted to do. Since then, I have created or negotiated most of the positions I have had, in order to be able to do the kind of work I was most interested in doing. I have had to do jobs that could barely cover my costs or work other jobs to support myself, but ultimately got to a great place in my career. Additionally, I am a single woman working in Latin America, which can be extremely sexist, and require the navigation of some strange and hidden gender norms.
column72: I have had several mentors, both male and female. I have been lucky enough to have female bosses for most of my professional life, as well. The most empowering moment was a co-worker who invited me to lunch my first week of my first full-time job, and started telling me about the importance of pushing for my own professional advancement. Without any prompting, she took it upon herself to give me advice on how to push for promotions, a raise, and bonuses. Because she knew many women did not advocate for themselves, she took the time to push me to do so and give me the tools and information to help me do so. I followed most of her advice then - and often continue to do so!

• My paternal grandfather was a colonel in the United States Army, and became an American citizen through his honorable military service. My father followed my grandfather?s military career path and became an army officer as well. He married my mother, a Philippine citizen, who naturalized soon after which makes me second generation American citizen on my father?s side and first generation on my mother?s. An army brat born and raised in Germany for 18 years, I learned how to adapt quickly to my surroundings by learning the German language, history, and culture while maintaining my proud American nationality and Filipino ethnicity. At home, we spoke English and Tagalog. In school and university, my sisters and I spoke German.
  • I studied German and Business at the University of Florida (UF). I was far from the small classrooms of the Department of Defense (DoDs) schools and one of 50,000+ students. Using skills cultivated as a military child, I adapted, made friends, and learned how to thrive in my new environment. I served as the German Club President and joined affinity groups for Filipino-American students; two ways to be involved and continue my connection with Germany and the Philippines.
  • After college, I worked for a chemical trading company in Florida. I honed my negotiation and communication skills in English and German. I learned complex warehouse management systems and how to organize and prioritize tasks. My supervisor worked with me to present myself and our company professionally to vendors and suppliers. I valued her efforts to make me a better associate. I fully understand how a mentor plays a significant part in professional development.
  • Just as my position with the chemical company was on the rise, the Department of State offered me a position. I responsibly left the company on good terms, and eagerly joined the Foreign Service - a personal goal of mine. The Foreign Service has taken me to Bolivia, Malawi, Iraq, Argentina, South Africa, Afghanistan, and Washington, D.C. I look forward to serving in Caracas, Venezuela in June 2018. Serving the U.S. government and representing our country abroad is my greatest joy. Proving that a ?true American? can be anything ? a German-speaking Filipino-American woman ? is one of my most treasured tasks. Again, I used those skills of adaptation and cultural awareness to be successful in foreign countries. I married a Foreign Service Officer, and we have a dog named Pancho that we adopted in South Africa. I love to cook, give cooking classes, read, and watch movies in my spare time.
  • I benefited so much from several male and female mentors in my 12-year diplomatic career, and continue to cultivate those relationships and build new ones every day. I look to them for advice, ask them to edit my writing, seek them out for personal and professional guidance,
  • and, most importantly, feel encouraged and empowered in my job. I look forward to providing that same positive influence for young women through Speak Mentorship.

column66: • Bio: I grew up as the youngest sibling in the Anderson family, spending all of my childhood in Brooklyn, NY.  My parents, who had immigrated from Jamaica prior to my birth, nurtured an early love for math and science and as former teachers, they always emphasized the importance of education.  Although reluctant to let me move far from home, my parents recognized the importance of following opportunities and allowed me to attend Stanford University despite their reservations. While pursuing a degree in human biology, I became fascinated with research but did not ultimately attempt research until after graduation. After moving back to the East Coast, I volunteered in the lab of Ann Gershon at Columbia University and was officially ?bitten? by the research bug. Since then, I went on to medical school where I again returned to research in the HHMI/NIH Research Scholars Program later translating the work during this year out fellowship into the basis for my thesis project for my PhD. My love for neurology and autoimmune diseases was fostered at that time and I?ve since entered fellowship at BWH/MGH in neuroimmunology where I get to continue pursuing my love of research, neurology and autoimmune disease.  Along the way, my path has been continuously guided by mentors, both formal and informal, and the importance of these relationships reminds me of the importance of becoming a mentor myself.

column67: I learnt about Speak Mentorship at an event in NYC organized by Women's Education Project. Having benefited from mentors in my life, i have always been a strong advocate of mentorship. I started formal mentorship in sophomore year of college when i became a mentor to first-year students at MIT. Even after i graduated, i have continued to run a mentorship program for young women at MIT. I find it so fulfilling to see the tangible impact I'm making in the lives of these young women. As a mentor you become a trusted advisor, and that's a huge responsibility and an honor at the same time. As a woman in STEM, i would absolutely love to take this opportunity to encourage young girls and women to not shy away from this field, but to join it whole-heartedly! Having grown up in south asia, i often saw young girls get discouraged by families. Now that i am an engineer, I want to use my position to impart confidence and passion in young girls for this field!
column68: I studied engineering at MIT, and now i work as a consultant at a leading management consulting firm. Traditionally i have seen while schools and colleges put emphasis on honing your quantitative skills, not as much emphasis is put on polishing the soft skills (i.e. communication and managerial), skills that are critical to go up the ladder in any field. This is why i decided to work at a consulting firm out of engineering college, because i wanted to bridge this gap. Having developed both strong quantitative and soft skills, i hope to impart these in my mentee because i believe the combination of both in a woman is powerful.
column69: Want to build a strong bond with my mentee, start a relationship of mentorship that lasts a long time! I want to make a tangible impact in the life of my mentee by positioning her to succeed both professionally and personally. Being some years older, I hope to help navigate her and also share the lessons i learnt through my journey in school and college. And most importantly i hope to gain a friend, somebody who trusts that id always have her best interest at heart.
column70: As a consultant it's critical to have the drive to take initiative and lead, to be a strong problem solver, to have the ability to communicate and socialize with clients from various industries. I would directly impact strong quantitative skills and soft skills (i.e. how to communicate with seniors vs. peers). In terms of hard skills, i could teach excel modeling and powerpoint. In consulting powerpoint slides is our bread and butter. It's so important to be able to storyteller and communicate effectively and that's what slide making teaches.
column71: As an engineer, school always put more emphasis on quantitative skills but not as much on soft skills. So i had to work extra hard to build these soft / managerial skills to prepare myself for a career in consulting.
column72: I have always had mentors throughout every phase of my life. An empowering moment would have to be when i first joined MIT. First few months were extremely tough, not only academically but also mentally. I was so far away from home (dubai), i couldn't tolerate the cold and of course college life can be extremely tough. I put on a lot of weight my first year, started eating really unhealthy and my grades in the beginning started to suffer. I was also never mentally at ease. But i had this amazing friend, confidante, mentor (a year older than me) who really helped me pull through. She would take out time to coach me on how to be effective at studying, she taught my how to cook basic healthy meals, and even joined a dancing class with me (even though she never enjoyed dancing) to keep me company.

column71: Sexism, some racism, my parents are all immigrants from the Caribbean so they don't have generational wealth here, nor long, deep connections, there were financial obstacles and sometimes emotional health obstacles, all of which I learned and used mentors to overcome.
column72: I never had a formal longer term mentor like you are giving these children. However, I understood the importance early on (especially as a child of immigrants). I realized it could help me.. learn about the culture, norms and expectations I was born into in this country. So I sought out multiple different peers and elders at each stage of my life, who could guide me simultaneously. But no, I never had a formal mentor like this. I recall one woman who taught me that I'd need to find and strike a balance between what my parents expected of me, what I wanted for myself and what opportunities I had. That sounded simple, but it wasn't as 1st gen kids sometimes have very stringent things their parents want them to do -- that don't jive with success in the U.S. She guided me through it and I gained respect for myself in finding my own way while respecting my family as well.

In his model as a fine artist, he taught me courage, which I think is the best potential outcome of a mentoring program.

column66: • Karmen grew up in Hong Kong and moved to the U.S. when she was 16. She completed her college education in Virginia and moved to California to launch her career in tech. Now almost 9 years in the tech industry, Karmen has built a successful career at IBM. Currently a global sales executive for Data & AI, she leads the data warehousing and information architecture business globally at IBM. Along the way, she has also found her voice in an industry traditionally dominated by men ? she co-founded Women in Big Data in 2015, hosted numerous meetups for women in tech, and served as a speaker at various guest lectures and tech panels, strongly advocating for diversity and inclusion in the industry. Given the global nature of her role at IBM, where she constantly has to work and collaborate with clients and colleagues across the globe both remotely and in-person, Karmen has long been familiar with working in a virtual environment well before the pandemic hit. She looks forward to sharing her experiences and some best practices on how to stay productive, professional, and stand out in an increasingly virtual workforce and work environment in this upcoming seminar.
column67: As an immigrant who moved to the U.S. by myself at age 16, I understand how it could feel overwhelming, how it may be difficult to understand your role and identity in a new country, and find a sense of belonging. I'd like to be a positive influence and role model to an immigrant/1st generation girl who may be facing some of these challenges, and provide guidance, support, and mentorship by sharing my experience and advice.

column71: I grew up rather shy and introverted, and sales is a very people-oriented, communications-heavy career. I didn't feel like I necessarily had the right personality or charisma to be successful in this field of work. This is also a very male-dominated industry with only 17% of us being females. It is mostly dominated by middle-aged male who have been in the field for decades. It was not easy entering this realm being a millennial, a female, and an immigrant on top of that. There were many additional barriers and challenges, false perceptions of who I am, what I represent, and what I am capable of etc that I had to overcome in order to be as successful as my male counterparts.
column72: Yes, I surrounded myself with a lot of mentors in college and also when I began my career at IBM.
